*HB1003.3*
Reprinted
January 30, 2026
HOUSE BILL No. 1003
_____
DIGEST OF HB 1003 (Updated January 29, 2026 2:41 pm - DI 92)
Citations Affected: Numerous provisions throughout the Indiana
Code.
Synopsis: Boards and commissions. Makes changes to requirements
for the readoption of administrative rules. Repeals, merges,
consolidates, or otherwise modifies various boards, commissions, and
other governmental bodies. Modifies or establishes various funds.
Makes changes to the membership, duties, and operations of various
boards, commissions, and other governmental bodies. Provides that the
professional licensing agency may adopt and enforce procedural rules
for the administration of a board if the rule: (1) will affect multiple
boards; and (2) is not inconsistent with any rule adopted by the affected
board. Repeals the fire prevention and building safety commission
(commission). Transfers the commission's responsibilities and
administrative rules to the department of homeland security
(department). Makes certain changes to the administration of building
and safety statutes and building and safety codes. Provides for the
codification of administrative rules setting forth building and safety
codes into statute. Makes an appropriation.
Effective: Upon passage; May 15, 2026; July 1, 2026; January 1,
2027.
Bartels, Miller D, Pierce K,
Dant Chesser
January 8, 2026, read first time and referred to Committee on Government and Regulatory
Reform.
January 22, 2026, amended, reported — Do Pass. Referred to Committee on Ways and
Means pursuant to Rule 126.3.
January 27, 2026, amended, reported — Do Pass.
January 29, 2026, read second time, amended, ordered engrossed.
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

Reprinted
January 30, 2026
Second Regular Session of the 124th General Assembly (2026)
PRINTING CODE. Amendments: Whenever an existing statute (or a section of the Indiana
Constitution) is being amended, the text of the existing provision will appear in this style type,
additions will appear in this style type, and deletions will appear in this style type.
Additions: Whenever a new statutory provision is being enacted (or a new constitutional
provision adopted), the text of the new provision will appear in this style type. Also, the
word NEW will appear in that style type in the introductory clause of each SECTION that adds
a new provision to the Indiana Code or the Indiana Constitution.
Conflict reconciliation: Text in a statute in this style type or this style type reconciles conflicts
between statutes enacted by the 2025 Regular Session of the General Assembly.
HOUSE BILL No. 1003
A BILL FOR AN ACT to amend the Indiana Code concerning state
and local administration and to make an appropriation.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the State of Indiana:

37 Chapter 53.5. Government Reform Task Force
38 Sec. 1. As used in this chapter, "agency" has the meaning set
39 forth in IC 4-22-2-3.
40 Sec. 2. As used in this chapter, "council" refers to the legislative
41 council established by IC 2-5-1.1-1.
42 Sec. 3. As used in this chapter, "rule" has the meaning set forth
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

8
1 in IC 4-22-2-3.
2 Sec. 4. As used in this chapter, "task force" refers to the
3 government reform task force established by section 5 of this
4 chapter.
5 Sec. 5. The government reform task force is established.
6 Sec. 6. (a) Except as provided in subsections (b), (c), (d), and (e),
7 IC 2-5-1.2 applies to the task force.
8 (b) The task force consists of the following ten (10) members of
9 the general assembly:
10 (1) Three (3) members appointed by the president pro
11 tempore of the senate.
12 (2) Two (2) members appointed by the minority leader of the
13 senate.
14 (3) Three (3) members appointed by the speaker of the house
15 of representatives.
16 (4) Two (2) members appointed by the minority leader of the
17 house of representatives.
18 A member of the task force serves at the pleasure of the appointing
19 authority.
20 (c) The task force shall meet as called by the chair. All meetings
21 of the task force shall be open to the public in accordance with and
22 subject to IC 5-14-1.5. All records of the task force shall be subject
23 to the requirements of IC 5-14-3.
24 (d) A majority of the members of the task force constitutes a
25 quorum. The affirmative vote of at least a majority of the members
26 at a meeting at which a quorum is present is necessary for the task
27 force to take official action other than to meet and take testimony.
28 (e) The:
29 (1) chairman of the council shall designate the chair; and
30 (2) vice chairman of the council shall designate the vice chair;
31 of the task force from the members of the task force. The chair and
32 vice chair of the task force serve as chair and vice chair at the
33 pleasure of the appointing authority.
34 Sec. 7. The task force shall study:
35 (1) the feasibility of transferring the administration of the
36 office of the state chemist from Purdue University to the
37 Indiana state department of agriculture;
38 (2) any employment issue arising from a transfer of the
39 administration of the office of the state chemist; and
40 (3) any issue arising from a transfer of any property, records,
41 funds, or accounts of the office of state chemist to the Indiana
42 state department of agriculture.
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

9
1 Sec. 8. The task force may request information or testimony
2 from department or agency heads, or the designee of a department
3 or agency head, as determined necessary by the task force.
4 Sec. 9. The task force shall:
5 (1) develop recommendations in a report concerning the
6 issues set forth in section 7 of this chapter; and
7 (2) submit the report to the council in an electronic format
8 under IC 5-14-6 not later than November 1, 2026.
9 Sec. 10. The legislative services agency shall provide support
10 staff to the task force.
11 Sec. 11. This chapter expires June 30, 2027.

12 SECTION 9. IC 4-15-12-8, AS AMENDED BY P.L.215-2016,
13 SECTION 88, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
14 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 8. (a) There is created the affirmative action
15 advisory committee to assist in the effective implementation of the
16 affirmative action policy. The committee is composed of eight (8)
17 members. The governor shall appoint the members of the committee
18 with the advice of the affirmative action officer. The members serve at
19 the pleasure of the governor.
20 (b) A member of the committee is entitled to reimbursement for
21 traveling expenses and other expenses actually incurred in connection
22 with the member's duties, as provided in the state travel policies and
23 procedures established by the department of administration and
24 approved by the budget agency. A member who is not an officer or
25 employee of the state is entitled to the minimum salary per diem as
26 provided in IC 4-10-11-2.1(b) while performing the member's duties.
27 (c) The committee shall select from its membership a chairperson
28 and vice chairperson to serve for one (1) year from the date of
29 selection. They may be reelected at the pleasure of the committee. In
30 any instance where the chairperson or vice chairperson does not serve
31 the chairperson's or vice chairperson's full term, the committee shall
32 select another to serve in the chairperson's or vice chairperson's own
33 right a full term.
34 (d) The affirmative action advisory committee shall:
35 (1) provide liaison activities with the affirmative action officer
36 with respect to problems and suggestions concerning the
37 affirmative action policy;
38 (2) advise the affirmative action officer and the governor of
39 recommended changes in the implementation of the affirmative
40 action policy and improved guidelines for state agency programs;
41 and
42 (3) advise the governor and the affirmative action officer

12
1 concerning the effectiveness and status of the total
2 implementation of the affirmative action policy.
3 (e) The affirmative action advisory committee may review the

17 SECTION 11. IC 4-22-2.6-4, AS AMENDED BY P.L.213-2025,
18 SECTION 56, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
19 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 4. (a) To readopt a rule, an agency must conduct
20 a review of the rule to consider the continued need for the rule and
21 whether the rule, if readopted, will meet each of the standards in
22 IC 4-22-2-19.5 and (if applicable) the requirements for fees, fines, and
23 civil penalties in IC 4-22-2-19.6.
24 (b) In the review, the agency shall reexamine previous cost benefit,
25 economic impact, fiscal impact, and regulatory burden statements
26 prepared by the agency for the rule under IC 4-3-22-13, IC 4-22-2-22.7,
27 IC 4-22-2-22.8, IC 4-22-2-28, IC 4-22-2.1-5, or an executive order and
28 revise the statements to reflect any change in circumstances that affect
29 the analysis. The agency shall identify any alternative methods of
30 achieving the purpose of the rule that are less costly or less intrusive,
31 or that would otherwise minimize the economic impact of the proposed
32 rule on small businesses (as defined in IC 4-22-2.1-4) and other
33 regulated entities. The agency also shall consider the following:
34 (1) The nature of any complaints or comments received from the
35 public, including small businesses (as defined in IC 4-22-2.1-4),
36 concerning the rule or the rule's implementation by the agency.
37 (2) The complexity of the rule, including any difficulties
38 encountered by:
39 (A) the agency in administering the rule; or
40 (B) small businesses (as defined in IC 4-22-2.1-4) or other
41 regulated persons in complying with the rule.
42 (3) The degree to which technology, economic conditions, or
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

13
1 other factors have changed in the area affected by the rule since
2 the last time the rule was reviewed.
3 (c) In the review, the agency shall compare the requirements
4 within the rule to similar requirements in Illinois, Kentucky,

6 of management and budget for comparison.
7 (c) (d) The agency shall prepare written findings concerning the
8 agency's determinations under this section. The written findings shall

10 (1) A statement identifying whether the program or subject
11 matter covered by the rule is still carried out by the agency.

19 (4) Either of the following:
20 (A) Any revisions to previously prepared cost benefit,
21 economic impact, fiscal impact, or regulatory burden
22 statements prepared by the agency for the rule under:
23 (i) IC 4-3-22-13;
24 (ii) IC 4-22-2-22.7;
25 (iii) IC 4-22-2-22.8;
26 (iv) IC 4-22-2-28; or
27 (v) IC 4-22-2.1-5;
28 if those previously prepared statements were published by
29 the Indiana Register.
30 (B) If the rule did not have a prior cost benefit, economic
31 impact, fiscal impact, or regulatory burden statement
32 prepared by the agency for the rule under:
33 (i) IC 4-3-22-13;
34 (ii) IC 4-22-2-22.7;
35 (iii) IC 4-22-2-22.8;
36 (iv) IC 4-22-2-28; or
37 (v) IC 4-22-2.1-5;
38 published in the Indiana Register, a copy of an updated
39 regulatory burden statement that meets the requirements
40 of IC 4-22-2-22.7.
41 (5) Any alternative methods of achieving the purpose of the
42 rule that are less costly or less intrusive, or that would
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

14
1 otherwise minimize the economic impact of the proposed rule
2 on small businesses (as defined in IC 4-22-2.1-4) and other
3 regulated entities.
4 (6) The nature of any complaints or comments received from
5 the public, including small businesses (as defined in
6 IC 4-22-2.1-4), concerning the rule or the rule's
7 implementation by the agency.
8 (7) Any difficulties encountered by:
9 (A) the agency in administering the rule; or
10 (B) small businesses (as defined in IC 4-22-2.1-4) or other
11 regulated persons in complying with the rule.
12 (8) The degree to which technology, economic conditions, or
13 other factors have changed in the area affected by the rule
14 since the last time the rule was adopted, readopted, or
15 amended.
16 (9) Whether the federal government or any of the states
17 covered by subsection (c) have less restrictive requirements
18 than the rule, and, if so, the evidence or unique circumstances
19 that justify why the more restrictive requirements in the rule
20 are necessary.
21 (10) The last time the substantive content of the rule was
22 amended.
23 (11) Whether the substantive content in the rule would be
24 more appropriately integrated into the Indiana Code as
25 opposed to remaining as a separate administrative rule. In
26 making such a determination, the agency shall consider the
27 frequency of updates to the rule since its initial promulgation.
28 If the substantive content of the rule has not been modified in
29 the prior eight (8) years, the agency must present specific
30 facts that justify keeping the substantive content in an
31 administrative rule rather than the Indiana Code.
32 (e) The written findings in subsection (d) must be submitted in
33 a form that can be easily loaded into commonly used business
34 analysis software and published in the Indiana Register using the
35 format jointly developed by the publisher, the office of
36 management and budget, and the budget agency. The office of
37 management and budget may provide more stringent requirements
38 for rules with fiscal impacts and costs above a threshold amount
39 determined by the office of management and budget.

31 of the specific powers and duties in this chapter does not exclude the
32 state library from engaging in any other activity, not contrary to law,
33 that the Indiana library and historical board may consider appropriate
34 in the development of library service to state government, to the
35 libraries and library profession of Indiana, and to the citizens of the
36 state.

5 SECTION 8, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
6 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 39.1. (a) The state library advisory council is
7 established for the purpose of advising the board and the state librarian
8 concerning:
9 (1) general policies of the state library;
10 (2) plans or programs for library development and interlibrary
11 cooperation;
12 (3) library research;
13 (4) professional development for librarians;
14 (5) standards and rules for library services;
15 (6) administration and distribution of state and federal funds; and
16 (7) other matters as requested by the board and the state librarian.
17 (b) The advisory council consists of not more than fifteen (15)
18 members.
19 (c) The board shall appoint the members of the advisory council,
20 with nominations for appointment from library organizations and the
21 state librarian.
22 (d) Members of the advisory council shall serve two (2) year terms.
23 However, the board shall stagger the terms of the initial appointees.
24 (e) Notwithstanding subsection (d), if a member misses a majority
25 of the advisory council's meetings in a calendar year, the board may
26 remove the member and appoint a new member to serve the remainder
27 of the term of the member removed under this subsection.
28 (f) A member of the advisory council is not entitled to
29 compensation, per diem, or reimbursement for expenses.
30 (g) A quorum of the members must be present for the advisory
31 council to take any official action. A quorum of the advisory council
32 consists of a majority of the members appointed to the advisory
33 council. An affirmative vote by a majority of the members present is
34 needed for the advisory council to make a recommendation or take any
35 official action.
36 (h) This section expires December 31, 2026.

14 SECTION 39. IC 4-23-7.2-11 IS REPEALED [EFFECTIVE JULY
15 1, 2026]: Sec. 11. (a) The historical bureau shall establish the Indiana
16 historical marker program for marking historical sites in Indiana. As a
17 part of this program, the historical bureau shall fix a state format for
18 historical markers. No person may erect an historical marker in the
19 state format without the approval of the historical bureau. All historical
20 markers in the state format shall be provided by the historical bureau
21 using appropriated funds, local matching funds, donations, grants, or
22 any other funds provided for that purpose according to the guidelines
23 and rules of the historical marker program.
24 (b) The board may appoint a historical marker advisory committee
25 to serve without compensation. The committee may advise the board
26 and the director concerning the following:
27 (1) Guidelines and rules for the historical marker program.
28 (2) Appropriate sites to be marked.
29 (3) Other matters concerning the historical marker program as
30 requested by the board or the director.
31 (c) Historical markers approved under this section, including state
32 format markers installed after 1945 and markers installed by the
33 Indiana Civil War centennial commission, are the property of the state.
34 Maintenance of state historical markers is part of the historical marker
35 program. The historical bureau may cooperate with individuals, local
36 and state agencies, and private institutions and organizations for the
37 maintenance of the historical markers. Funds made available to the
38 historical marker program, as approved by the board, may be used for
39 necessary maintenance.
40 (d) No historical marker may be erected on a highway of the state
41 highway system without the approval of the historical bureau as to its
42 historical accuracy. This provision is in addition to any other
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

28
1 requirement of law.

5 SECTION 57. IC 4-23-35 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA CODE
6 AS A NEW CHAPTER TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
7 JULY 1, 2026]:
8 Chapter 35. Indiana State Historical Records Advisory Board
9 Sec. 1. As used in this chapter, "board" refers to the Indiana
10 state historical records advisory board established by section 3 of
11 this chapter.
12 Sec. 2. As used in this chapter, "NHPRC" means the federal
13 National Historical Publications and Records Commission.
14 Sec. 3. The Indiana state historical records advisory board:
15 (1) initially established under Executive Order 06-01; and
16 (2) continued under Executive Order 13-13 and 17-06;
17 is established under the Indiana department of administration. The
18 board is a continuing board. The purpose of the board is to carry
19 out the duties under section 8 of this chapter.
20 Sec. 4. (a) The board consists of seven (7) voting members.
21 (b) At least four (4) members of the board must have recognized
22 experience in the administration of government records, historical
23 records, or archives.
24 (c) One (1) of the seven (7) members under subsection (a) must
25 be the state archivist. The remaining six (6) members shall be
26 appointed by the state archivist and are lay members who:
27 (1) may be reappointed; and
28 (2) serve at the will of the state archivist.
29 There is no term limit for members of the board.
30 (d) The state archivist shall serve as state coordinator and chair
31 of the board.
32 Sec. 5. (a) The term of a member appointed under section 4(a)
33 of this chapter is three (3) years.
34 (b) The state archivist shall appoint a new member to fill a
35 vacancy on the board that occurs for any reason. A member
36 appointed under this subsection serves the remainder of the
37 unexpired term of the vacating member.
38 Sec. 6. (a) This section applies to a member of the board
39 appointed under Executive Order 06-01, 13-13, or 17-06.
40 (b) An individual who serves as a member of the board on June
41 30, 2026, is appointed by operation of law to serve the remainder
42 of the member's unexpired term. A subsequent vacancy shall be
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

34
1 filled by the state archivist under section 4(c) of this chapter.
2 (c) This section expires July 1, 2030.
3 Sec. 7. (a) The board shall meet at least quarterly at the call of
4 the chair.
5 (b) The chair may call additional meetings. There is no
6 maximum number of meetings that may be called by the chair.
7 (c) A quorum consists of a simple majority of voting members.
8 Sec. 8. (a) The board shall serve as the central advisory body
9 for:
10 (1) historical records planning; and
11 (2) projects:
12 (A) funded by the NHPRC; and
13 (B) developed and carried out within Indiana.
14 (b) The board may do the following:
15 (1) Develop and submit to the NHPRC state priorities for
16 historical records programs as part of a state plan.
17 (2) Solicit or develop proposals for NHPRC grant projects.
18 (3) Review proposals by institutions in Indiana and make
19 recommendations about the proposals to the NHPRC.
20 (4) Work to preserve Indiana's documentary heritage.
21 (5) Promote practices that ensure preservation of, and access
22 to, the state's public and private records.
23 (6) Encourage adherence to archival and records management
24 principles through meetings and workshops.
25 (7) Survey repositories, support the state's records
26 management program, and promote the sharing of collection
27 information.
28 (8) Encourage the professional development of archivists,
29 curators, volunteers, and others dedicated to the task of
30 caring for Indiana's documentary heritage.
31 Sec. 9. (a) Each member of the board who is not a state
32 employee is not entitled to the minimum salary per diem provided
33 by IC 4-10-11-2.1(b). A member is, however, entitled to
34 reimbursement for expenses actually incurred in connection with
35 the member's duties as provided in the state policies and
36 procedures established by the Indiana department of
37 administration and approved by the budget agency.
38 (b) The expenses of the board shall be paid in the same manner
39 in which the board's expenses were paid under Executive Orders
40 06-01, 13-13, and 17-06.
41 (c) The Indiana department of administration shall staff the
42 board.
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

35
1 Sec. 10. The affirmative votes of a majority of the members
2 appointed to the board are required for the board to take action on
3 any measure, including final reports.
4 Sec. 11. (a) The board shall annually submit a report on its work
5 to the governor.
6 (b) A report under subsection (a) is a public record.
7 SECTION 58. IC 4-23-36 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA CODE
8 AS A NEW CHAPTER TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
9 JULY 1, 2026]:
10 Chapter 36. Indiana Cultural Commission
11 Sec. 1. As used in this chapter, "commission" refers to the
12 Indiana cultural commission established by section 3 of this
13 chapter.
14 Sec. 2. As used in this chapter, "Native American Indian" means
15 an individual who is at least one (1) of the following:
16 (1) An Alaska native as defined in 43 U.S.C. 1602(b).
17 (2) An Indian as defined in 25 U.S.C. 450b(d).
18 (3) A native Hawaiian as defined in 20 U.S.C. 7517(1).
19 Sec. 3. The Indiana cultural commission is established.
20 Sec. 4. (a) The commission consists of fifteen (15) members
21 appointed as follows:
22 (1) Two (2) members of the house of representatives
23 appointed as follows:
24 (A) One (1) member appointed by the speaker of the house
25 of representatives.
26 (B) One (1) member appointed by the minority leader of
27 the house of representatives.
28 (2) Two (2) members of the senate appointed as follows:
29 (A) One (1) member appointed by the president pro
30 tempore of the senate.
31 (B) One (1) member appointed by the minority leader of
32 the senate.
33 (3) One (1) individual who is not a member of the general
34 assembly appointed by the speaker of the house of
35 representatives.
36 (4) One (1) individual who is not a member of the general
37 assembly appointed by the president pro tempore of the
38 senate.
39 (5) The secretary of the family and social services
40 administration, or the secretary's designee.
41 (6) The commissioner of the Indiana department of health, or
42 the commissioner's designee.
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

36
1 (7) The secretary of education, or the secretary's designee.
2 (8) The commissioner of the department of correction, or the
3 commissioner's designee.
4 (9) The director of the civil rights commission, or the
5 director's designee.
6 (10) Four (4) individuals appointed by the governor in
7 accordance with subsection (b).
8 (b) The following apply to members of the commission
9 appointed by the governor under subsection (a)(10):
10 (1) The appointed individuals may not be members of the
11 general assembly or state employees.
12 (2) The appointed individuals must represent the following:
13 (A) African Americans.
14 (B) The Hispanic/Latino community.
15 (C) Women.
16 (D) Native American Indians.
17 (c) If a legislative member of the commission ceases to be a
18 member of the legislative chamber from which the member was
19 appointed, the member ceases to be a member of the commission.
20 (d) A member of the commission:
21 (1) serves at the pleasure of the member's appointing
22 authority; and
23 (2) may be removed at any time by the appointing authority.
24 (e) If a vacancy on the commission occurs, the appointing
25 authority who appointed the former member whose position has
26 become vacant shall appoint an individual to fill the vacancy. An
27 individual appointed to fill a vacancy serves on the commission for
28 the remainder of the unexpired term of the individual's
29 predecessor.
30 Sec. 5. (a) A member of the commission may be reappointed for
31 successive terms.
32 (b) The following members serve a two (2) year term that
33 expires June 30 of an odd-numbered year:
34 (1) A member of the general assembly appointed to the
35 commission under section 4 of this chapter.
36 (2) A member appointed to the commission under section
37 4(a)(3) or 4(a)(4) of this chapter.
38 (c) A member appointed to the commission under section
39 4(a)(10) of this chapter serves a term that expires December 31,
40 2028, and each fourth year thereafter.
41 Sec. 6. (a) The commission shall meet:
42 (1) at the call of the chairperson as necessary to fulfill its
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

37
1 duties under this chapter; and
2 (2) at least once quarterly in each calendar year.
3 (b) The chairperson shall designate the following subcommittees
4 to meet at least two (2) times each year:
5 (1) African American affairs.
6 (2) Hispanic/Latino affairs.
7 (3) Women and families.
8 (4) Native American Indian affairs.
9 Each subcommittee shall submit an annual report on its activities
10 to the full commission.
11 (c) The chairperson may:
12 (1) designate other subcommittees to meet between
13 commission meetings; and
14 (2) require a subcommittee to report on its activities to the full
15 commission.
16 (d) A subcommittee designated under this section is comprised
17 of the following:
18 (1) At least three (3) members of the commission appointed by
19 the chairperson.
20 (2) Not more than three (3) members of the public appointed
21 by the chairperson.
22 A member of public appointed to a subcommittee must be selected
23 based on the individual's background and experience related to the
24 subcommittee's designated purpose.
25 (e) A member of the public who serves on a subcommittee is
26 entitled to receive a minimum salary per diem and to
27 reimbursement for mileage and traveling expenses. Amounts
28 payable under this subsection must be paid:
29 (1) from appropriations made to the civil rights commission;
30 and
31 (2) in the same manner that a per diem, mileage, and traveling
32 expenses are paid to a member of the commission described
33 in section 9(a) of this chapter.
34 Sec. 7. (a) At the first official meeting of the commission each
35 year, the members shall elect:
36 (1) a member to serve as the chairperson of the commission;
37 and
38 (2) a member to serve as the vice chairperson of the
39 commission.
40 (b) A member elected to serve as chairperson or vice
41 chairperson of the commission shall serve for a term of one (1)
42 year. The term of office expires at the first commission meeting
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

38
1 held in the following year.
2 (c) A vacancy in the office of chairperson or vice chairperson
3 must be filled by a member of the commission selected by vote of
4 the remaining members of the commission.
5 Sec. 8. Eight (8) members of the commission constitute a
6 quorum for conducting commission business. The affirmative vote
7 of at least eight (8) members is required for the commission to take
8 official action on any measure. A member of the commission may
9 participate in a meeting by electronic means in accordance with
10 IC 5-14-1.5-3.6 as long as at least five (5) members of the
11 commission are physically present at the meeting.
12 Sec. 9. (a) Each member of the commission who is not a state
13 employee is entitled to the minimum salary per diem provided by
14 IC 4-10-11-2.1(b). The member is also entitled to reimbursement
15 for mileage and traveling expenses as provided under IC 4-13-1-4,
16 and other expenses actually incurred in connection with the
17 member's duties as provided in the state policies and procedures
18 established by the Indiana department of administration and
19 approved by the budget agency.
20 (b) Each member of the commission who is a state employee is
21 entitled to reimbursement for traveling expenses as provided under
22 IC 4-13-1-4 and other expenses actually incurred in connection
23 with the member's duties as provided in the state policies and
24 procedures established by the Indiana department of
25 administration and approved by the budget agency.
26 (c) Each member of the commission who is a member of the
27 general assembly is entitled to receive the same per diem, mileage,
28 and travel allowances paid to members of the general assembly
29 serving on interim study committees established by the legislative
30 council. Per diem, mileage, and travel allowances paid under this
31 subsection shall be paid from appropriations made to the
32 legislative council or the legislative services agency.
33 (d) Expenses paid under subsections (a) and (b) shall be paid
34 from appropriations made to the civil rights commission.
35 Sec. 10. The commission shall do the following:
36 (1) Identify, study, and research issues affecting
37 underrepresented and minority groups, including the groups
38 described in section 6(b) of this chapter, in the following
39 areas:
40 (A) Health care.
41 (B) Economic and business development.
42 (C) Employment.
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

39
1 (D) Education.
2 (E) Social and family development.
3 (F) Housing.
4 (G) Civil rights.
5 (H) The justice system.
6 (I) Any other aspect of society identified by the
7 commission.
8 (2) Promote cooperation and understanding between
9 communities throughout Indiana.
10 (3) Assess the needs of underrepresented and minority groups
11 and identify recommendations to resolve those needs.
12 (4) Promote full participation of underrepresented and
13 minority groups in all aspects of society.
14 (5) Identify legal and social barriers to underrepresented and
15 minority groups.
16 (6) Identify measures that could facilitate easier access to
17 state and local government services by underrepresented and
18 minority groups.

21 Native American Indian historical and archeological sites,
22 including the reburial of remains of Native American Indians,
23 that are consistent with IC 14-21-1-25.5.
24 (8) Monitor legislation, policies, and other legal developments
25 in order to make recommendations to the general assembly
26 and the governor that support the commission's purposes.
27 (9) Gather, study, and disseminate information on
28 underrepresented and minority groups through publications,
29 public hearings, conferences, and other means.
30 (10) Before November 1 of each year, submit an annual report
31 on the commission's activities to:

33 (B) the legislative council in an electronic format under
34 IC 5-14-6.

32 to the commission.

34 needed to meet the obligations of the fund in the same manner as
35 other public funds may be invested. Interest on investments made
36 under this subsection accrues to the fund.
37 (f) Money in the fund at the end of a state fiscal year does not
38 revert to the state general fund.
39 (g) Money in the fund is continuously appropriated for the
40 purposes described in subsection (a).

28 tested by, and hold a valid certification from, a facility that, or an
29 individual who, has been accredited by the American Welding
30 Society to test and certify welders and welding inspectors.

32 adequate welding standards and are listed in the project's job
33 specifications.
34 (3) All structural steel welding inspections listed in the project's
35 job specifications are completed by a certified welding inspector.

1 SECTION 90. IC 8-23-9-60, AS ADDED BY P.L.70-2023,
2 SECTION 2, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
3 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 60. (a) As used in this section, "adequate welding
4 standards" means specifications, guidelines, tests, and other methods
5 used to ensure that all structural steel welds meet, at minimum, the
6 codes and standards for such welds established in the welding code and
7 under rules adopted by the fire prevention and building safety
8 commission under IC 22-13-2-2. department of homeland security.
9 (b) As used in this section, "certified welding inspector" means a
10 person who has been certified by the American Welding Society to
11 inspect structural steel welding projects and conduct welder
12 qualification tests.
13 (c) As used in this section, "structural steel welding" means
14 structural welds, weld repair, the structural system, and the welding of
15 all primary steel members of a structure in accordance with the welding
16 code. The term does not include welding that is required by the
17 American Society of Mechanical Engineers to have its own
18 certification.
19 (d) As used in this section, "welding code" refers to the American
20 Welding Society structural steel welding code D1.1, as in effect on July
21 1, 2023.
22 (e) A contractor whose workers are welding the structural steel on
23 a project shall ensure that all of the following occur:
24 (1) The workers performing the structural steel welding have been
25 tested by, and hold a valid certification from, a facility that, or an
26 individual who, has been accredited by the American Welding
27 Society to test and certify welders and welding inspectors.
28 (2) All structural steel welds performed for the project meet
29 adequate welding standards and follow the project's special
30 provisions.
31 (3) All structural steel welding inspections listed in the project's
32 special provisions are completed by a certified welding inspector.
33 (f) If the department reasonably suspects a contractor has violated
34 this section, the department shall require the contractor to remedy the
35 violation not later than thirty (30) days after the department notifies the
36 contractor of the violation. The notification to the contractor must be
37 signed by the commissioner and sent by a method that enables the
38 department to verify receipt of the notice by the contractor. During the
39 thirty (30) day period, the contractor may continue to work on the
40 project. If the contractor fails to remedy the violation within the thirty
41 (30) day period, the department shall find the contractor not
42 responsible and determine the length of time the contractor is
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

58
1 considered not responsible by the department.
2 (g) In making the determination of the length of time a contractor is
3 not responsible under subsection (f), the department shall consider the
4 severity of the violation. The period during which a contractor is
5 considered not responsible:
6 (1) may not exceed forty-eight (48) months; and
7 (2) begins on the date of substantial completion of the project.
8 (h) A finding by the department under subsection (f) that a
9 contractor is not responsible may not be used by another state agency
10 or a political subdivision in making a determination as to whether the
11 contractor is responsible for purposes of that state agency's or political
12 subdivision's award of a public works contract to that contractor.

HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

63
1 that must be disseminated to both the fire prevention and building
2 safety commission and local authorities.
3 (6) Assistance to local officials in designing local emergency
4 action plans.
5 (7) Authorization and procedures for the erection or other
6 construction of temporary works designed to protect against or
7 mitigate danger, damage, or loss from flood, conflagration, or
8 other disaster.
9 (8) Preparation and distribution to the appropriate state and local
10 officials of state catalogs of federal, state, and private assistance
11 programs.
12 (9) Organization of manpower and chains of command.
13 (10) Coordination of federal, state, and local disaster activities.
14 (11) Coordination of the state disaster plan with the disaster plans
15 of the federal government.
16 (12) Other necessary matters.
17 (b) The agency shall take an integral part in the development and
18 revision of local and interjurisdictional disaster plans prepared under
19 section 17 of this chapter. The agency shall employ or otherwise secure
20 the services of professional and technical personnel capable of
21 providing expert assistance to political subdivisions, a political
22 subdivision's disaster agencies, and interjurisdictional planning and
23 disaster agencies. These personnel:
24 (1) shall consult with subdivisions and government agencies on
25 a regularly scheduled basis;
26 (2) shall make field examinations of the areas, circumstances, and
27 conditions to which particular local and interjurisdictional
28 disaster plans are intended to apply; and
29 (3) may suggest revisions.
30 (c) In preparing and revising the state disaster plan, the agency shall
31 seek the advice and assistance of local government, business, labor,
32 industry, agriculture, civic and volunteer organizations, and community
33 leaders. In advising local and interjurisdictional agencies, the agency
34 shall encourage local and interjurisdictional agencies to seek advice
35 from the sources specified in this subsection.
36 (d) The state disaster plan or any part of the plan may be
37 incorporated in rules of the agency or by executive orders.
38 (e) The agency shall do the following:
39 (1) Determine requirements of the state and political subdivisions
40 for food, clothing, and other necessities in the event of an
41 emergency.
42 (2) Procure and pre-position supplies, medicines, materials, and
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

64
1 equipment.
2 (3) Adopt standards and requirements for local and
3 interjurisdictional disaster plans.
4 (4) Provide for mobile support units.
5 (5) Assist political subdivisions, political subdivisions' disaster
6 agencies, and interjurisdictional disaster agencies to establish and
7 operate training programs and public information programs.
8 (6) Make surveys of industries, resources, and facilities in
9 Indiana, both public and private, necessary to carry out this
10 chapter.
11 (7) Plan and make arrangements for the availability and use of
12 any private facilities, services, and property, and if necessary and
13 if the private facilities, services, or property is used, provide for
14 payment for the use under agreed upon terms and conditions.
15 (8) Establish a register of persons with types of training and skills
16 important in emergency prevention, preparedness, response, and
17 recovery.
18 (9) Establish a register of mobile and construction equipment and
19 temporary housing available for use in a disaster emergency.
20 (10) Prepare, for issuance by the governor, executive orders,
21 proclamations, and regulations necessary or appropriate in coping
22 with disaster.
23 (11) Cooperate with the federal government and any public or
24 private agency or entity in achieving any purpose of this chapter
25 and in implementing programs for disaster prevention,
26 preparation, response, and recovery.
27 (12) Do other things necessary, incidental, or appropriate to
28 implement this chapter.
29 (f) The agency shall ascertain the rapid and efficient
30 communications that exist in times of disaster emergencies. The agency
31 shall consider the desirability of supplementing these communications
32 resources or of integrating these resources into a comprehensive
33 intrastate or state-federal telecommunications or other communications
34 system or network. In studying the character and feasibility of any
35 system, the agency shall evaluate the possibility of multipurpose use of
36 the system for general state and local governmental purposes. The
37 agency shall make appropriate recommendations to the governor.
38 (g) The agency shall assist political subdivisions in implementing
39 the intrastate mutual aid compact created by section 10.8 of this
40 chapter.

27 The state comptroller shall draw a warrant on the treasurer of state,
28 payable to the superintendent, engineers, elevator operators, and
29 watchmen, for the amounts due them as salaries and to the
30 superintendent for a total of expenditures other than salaries incurred
31 in the management of the monument and Monument Circle as shown
32 by the vouchers.

86
1 may belong to the same political party.

3 member shall hold over after the expiration of the member's term until

8 the same manner as the original appointment.

10 The board shall annually elect one (1) of its ex officio members as vice

21 member's duties.

24 connection with the member's duties.
25 (k) An ex officio member of the board may designate a person to
26 serve as an ex officio member of the board in the absence of the ex
27 officio member.
28 (l) The majority of the members of the board constitute a quorum for
29 the purposes of conducting the board's business and exercising the
30 board's powers and for all other purposes. Vacant positions may not be
31 counted when determining whether a majority of the members is
32 present.
33 (m) The affirmative vote of a majority of all the members of the
34 board who are present is necessary for the authority to take action. A
35 vacancy in the membership of the board does not impair the right of a
36 quorum to exercise all the rights and perform all the duties of the
37 authority. An action taken by the board under this article may be
38 authorized by:
39 (1) resolution at any regular or special meeting; or
40 (2) unanimous consent of all the members who have not
41 abstained.
42 A resolution takes effect immediately upon adoption and need not be

6 SECTION 148. IC 12-13-14-1, AS AMENDED BY P.L.210-2015,
7 SECTION 36, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
8 JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 1. (a) As used in this chapter, "automated
9 teller machine" means an electronic hardware device owned or
10 operated by or on behalf of a financial institution or retailer that is
11 capable of dispensing currency and responding to balance inquiries
12 through the use of a magnetic stripe card issued by or on behalf of the
13 division for distribution of assistance through an EBT system as
14 described in this chapter.
15 (b) As used in this chapter, "commission" refers to the electronic
16 benefits transfer commission established by this chapter.
17 (c) (b) As used in this chapter, "Department" refers to the United
18 States Department of Health and Human Services.
19 (d) (c) As used in this chapter, "EBT program" means an electronic
20 benefits transfer program.
21 (e) (d) As used in this chapter, "financial institution" means a bank,
22 trust company, savings institution, credit union, or any other
23 organization:
24 (1) whose principal business activity is providing banking or
25 financial services to the public; and
26 (2) that is organized, supervised, and authorized to do business in
27 Indiana under IC 28 or Title 12 of the United States Code.
28 (f) (e) As used in this chapter, "food retailer" means a retailer that:
29 (1) sells food items to consumers; and
30 (2) has been authorized under 7 CFR 278 to participate in SNAP.
31 (g) (f) As used in this chapter, "person" includes any individual or
32 entity described in IC 6-2.5-1-3.
33 (h) (g) As used in this chapter, "point of sale terminal" means an
34 electronic hardware device that is:
35 (1) used at a retailer's place of business where consumers pay for
36 goods or services; and
37 (2) capable of:
38 (A) initiating a request for authorization of a purchase of
39 tangible personal property;
40 (B) disbursing currency from an account;
41 (C) initiating a balance inquiry for an account; or
42 (D) distributing assistance through an EBT system as
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

88
1 described in this chapter.
2 (i) (h) As used in this chapter, "primary business" means more than
3 fifty percent (50%) of the gross retail income (as defined in
4 IC 6-2.5-1-5) attributable to the location or premises where the
5 business is located.
6 (j) (i) As used in this chapter, "retailer" means a person that, in the
7 ordinary course of business:
8 (1) sells or transfers tangible personal property; or
9 (2) provides or performs services for compensation;
10 to consumers.

20 (2) Documents that compliance with an alternative method of
21 compliance approved by the division will not be adverse to the

23 applicant for the variance, as determined by the division.
24 (c) A variance granted under subsection (b) must be conditioned
25 upon compliance with the alternative method approved by the division.
26 Noncompliance constitutes the violation of a rule of the division and
27 may be the basis for revoking the variance.

33 application for the waiver will create an undue hardship on the

36 substantial compliance with the rules adopted by the division after

6 one (1) battery operated smoke detector in each room and
7 corridor used by the ministry.
8 (B) Provide a notice on a form prescribed by the department
9 of homeland security to the parents of each child who attends
10 the ministry stating that the ministry does not have the same
11 level of fire safety protection as a licensed child care center.
12 (3) Each registered child care ministry shall comply with the rules
13 of the fire prevention and building safety commission
14 department of homeland security concerning fire drills.
15 For purposes of this subsection, occupant load is determined by
16 dividing the total square footage of the area used by the child care
17 ministry by thirty-five (35) and rounding any result that is not a whole
18 number up to the next whole number.
19 (d) The state fire marshal shall make an inspection of a child care
20 ministry registered under section 2 of this chapter at least annually.
21 (e) During an inspection, the state fire marshal shall inspect the
22 structure in which the child care ministry is conducted for fire safety
23 and life safety with respect to the structure's primary use.

42 establish the Indiana historical marker program for marking
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

111
1 historical sites in Indiana. As a part of this program, the review
2 board shall fix a state format for historical markers. No person
3 may erect a historical marker in the state format without the
4 approval of the review board. All historical markers in the state
5 format shall be provided by the review board using appropriated
6 funds, local matching funds, donations, grants, or any other funds

22 organizations for the maintenance of the historical markers. Funds

24 the review board, may be used for necessary maintenance.

26 state highway system without the approval of the review board as
27 to its historical accuracy. This provision is in addition to any other
28 requirement of law.

27 (c) Not later than thirty (30) days after receiving a request under
28 subsection (b), the department shall provide, by certified mail, written
29 notice of the request to the following:
30 (1) Each person with whom the commission holds a contract for:
31 (A) the provision of certain minimum quantities of stream
32 flow; or
33 (B) the sale of water on a unit pricing basis;
34 as of the date of the request.
35 (2) The executive and legislative body of each:
36 (A) county;
37 (B) municipality, if any; and
38 (C) conservancy district established under IC 14-33, if any;
39 in which the water sought in the request would be used.
40 (3) The executive and legislative body of each:
41 (A) county;
42 (B) municipality, if any; and
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

113
1 (C) conservancy district established under IC 14-33, if any;
2 in which the affected reservoir is located.
3 (d) Not later than seven (7) days after receiving a notice from the
4 department under subsection (c), each person described in subsection
5 (c)(1) shall, by certified mail, provide written notice of the request to
6 each:
7 (1) water utility; or
8 (2) other person;
9 that contracts with the person described in subsection (c)(1) for the
10 purchase of water for resale. Each person to whom notice is mailed
11 under this subsection is in turn responsible for providing written notice
12 by certified mail to each water utility or other person that purchases
13 water from that person for resale. A water utility or another person
14 required to provide notice under this subsection shall mail the required
15 notice not later than seven (7) days after it receives notice of the
16 request from the water utility or other person from whom it purchases
17 water for resale.
18 (e) At the same time that:
19 (1) a person described in subsection (c)(1); or
20 (2) a water utility or another person described in subsection (d);
21 mails any notice required under subsection (d), it shall also mail to the
22 department, by certified mail, a list of the names and addresses of each
23 water utility or other person to whom it has mailed the notice under
24 subsection (d).

27 IC 5-3-1, in each county:
28 (1) in which a person described in section (c)(1) is located;
29 (2) in which the affected reservoir is located;
30 (3) in which the water sought in the request would be used; and
31 (4) in which a water utility or other person included in a list
32 received by the department under subsection (e) is located.
33 Notwithstanding IC 5-3-1-6, in each county in which publication is
34 required under this subsection, notice shall be published in at least one
35 (1) general circulation newspaper in the county. The department may,
36 in its discretion, publish public notices in a qualified publication (as
37 defined in IC 5-3-1-0.7) or additional newspapers to provide
38 supplementary notification to the public. The cost of publishing
39 supplementary notification is a proper expenditure of the department.
40 (g) A notice required to be mailed or published under this section
41 must:
42 (1) identify the person making the request;
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

114
1 (2) include a brief description of:
2 (A) the nature of the pending request; and

38 SECTION 202. IC 14-34-10-2 IS AMENDED TO READ AS
39 FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 2. (a) As used in
40 this section, "higher or better uses" means postmining land uses that
41 have a higher:
42 (1) economic value; or
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

123
1 (2) nonmonetary benefit;
2 to the landowner or the community than the premining land uses.
3 (b) In addition to other standards a permittee must meet under rules
4 of the commission, a permittee shall do the following:
5 (1) Place markers on the site to readily identify the permit area.
6 (2) Conduct the surface coal mining operation in a manner that
7 maximizes the use and conservation of the solid fuel resource that
8 is recovered so that reaffecting the land in the future through
9 surface coal mining is minimized.
10 (3) Restore the land affected to a condition capable of supporting
11 the uses that the land was capable of supporting before mining or
12 higher or better uses of which there is a reasonable likelihood if:
13 (A) those uses do not:
14 (i) present an actual or a probable hazard to public health or
15 safety; or
16 (ii) pose an actual or a probable threat of water diminution
17 or pollution; and
18 (B) the permit applicant's declared proposed land use
19 following reclamation:
20 (i) is not impractical or unreasonable;
21 (ii) is not inconsistent with applicable land use policies and
22 plans;
23 (iii) does not involve unreasonable delay in implementation;
24 or
25 (iv) does not violate federal, state, or local law.
26 (4) Except as provided in subdivisions (5) and (6) and section 4
27 of this chapter with respect to all surface coal mining operations
28 backfill, compact where advisable to ensure stability or prevent
29 the leaching of toxic materials, and grade to restore the
30 approximate original contour of the land with all highwalls, spoil
31 piles, and depressions eliminated. Small depressions are allowed
32 if needed to retain moisture to assist revegetation or as otherwise
33 authorized under this article.
34 (5) In a surface coal mining operation that:
35 (A) is carried out at the same location over a substantial time;
36 (B) transects the coal deposit and the thickness of the coal
37 deposit relative to the volume of the overburden that is large;
38 and
39 (C) has overburden and other spoil and waste materials at a
40 particular point in the permit area or otherwise available from
41 the entire permit area that is insufficient, giving due
42 consideration to volumetric expansion, to restore the
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

124
1 approximate original contour;
2 the operator, at a minimum, shall backfill, grade, and compact,
3 where advisable, using all available overburden and other spoil
4 and waste materials to attain the lowest practicable grade but not
5 more than the angle of repose to provide adequate drainage and
6 to cover all acid-forming and other toxic materials to achieve an
7 ecologically sound land use compatible with the surrounding
8 region.
9 (6) If in surface coal mining:
10 (A) the volume of overburden is large relative to the thickness
11 of the coal deposit; and
12 (B) the operator demonstrates that due to volumetric
13 expansion the amount of overburden and other spoil and waste
14 materials removed in the course of the mining operation is
15 more than sufficient to restore the approximate original
16 contour;
17 the operator shall, after restoring the approximate contour,
18 backfill, grade, and compact, where advisable, the excess
19 overburden and other spoil and waste materials to attain the
20 lowest grade but not more than the angle of repose and to cover
21 all acid-forming and other toxic materials to achieve an
22 ecologically sound land use compatible with the surrounding
23 region. The overburden or spoil shall be shaped and graded in a
24 way that prevents slides, erosion, and water pollution and
25 revegetated in accordance with the requirements of this article.
26 (7) Stabilize and protect all surface areas, including spoil piles,
27 affected by the surface coal mining and reclamation operation to
28 effectively control erosion and attendant air and water pollution.
29 (8) Remove the topsoil from the land in a separate layer and:
30 (A) replace the topsoil on the backfill area; or
31 (B) if the topsoil is not used immediately;
32 (i) segregate the topsoil in a separate pile from other spoil;
33 and
34 (ii) if the topsoil is not replaced on a backfill area within a
35 time short enough to avoid deterioration of the topsoil,
36 maintain a successful cover by quick growing plants or other
37 means so that the topsoil is preserved from wind and water
38 erosion, remains free of any contamination by other acid or
39 toxic material, and is in a usable condition for sustaining
40 vegetation when restored during reclamation.
41 However, if the topsoil is of insufficient quantity or of poor
42 quality to sustain vegetation or if other strata are more suitable for
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

125
1 vegetation requirements, the operator shall remove, segregate, and
2 preserve, in a like manner, the strata that are best able to support
3 vegetation.
4 (9) Restore the topsoil or the best available subsoil that is best
5 able to support vegetation.
6 (10) For all prime farmland as identified in IC 14-34-3-3(16),
7 comply with the specifications for soil removal, storage,
8 replacement, and reconstruction established by rules of the
9 commission and do the following:
10 (A) Segregate the A horizon of the natural soil unless it is
11 shown that other available soil materials will create a final soil
12 that has a greater productive capacity, stockpile this material,
13 if not used immediately, separately from other spoil, and
14 provide needed protection from wind and water erosion or
15 contamination by other acid or toxic material.
16 (B) Segregate the B horizon of the natural soil, or underlying
17 C horizons or other strata, or a combination of those horizons
18 or other strata that are texturally and chemically suitable for
19 plant growth and equal to or more favorable for plant growth
20 than the B horizon, in sufficient quantities to create in the
21 regraded final soil a root zone of comparable depth and quality
22 to that existing in the natural soil, stockpile this material, if not
23 used immediately, separately from other spoil, and provide
24 needed protection from wind and water erosion or
25 contamination by other acid or toxic material.
26 (C) Replace and regrade the root zone material described in
27 clause (B) with proper compaction and uniform depth over the
28 regraded spoil material.
29 (D) Redistribute and grade in a uniform manner the surface
30 soil horizon described in clause (A).
31 (11) Create, if authorized in the approved surface coal mining and
32 reclamation plan, permanent impoundments of water on mining
33 sites. The permittee may create the permanent impoundment only
34 after the permittee demonstrates the following:
35 (A) The size of the impoundment is adequate for the intended
36 purposes.
37 (B) The impoundment dam construction will be designed to
38 achieve necessary stability with an adequate margin of safety
39 compatible with that of structures constructed under 16 U.S.C.
40 1006.
41 (C) The quality of impounded water will be suitable, on a
42 permanent basis, for the intended use and discharges from the
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

126
1 impoundment will not degrade the water quality below water
2 quality standards established under applicable federal and
3 state law in the receiving stream.
4 (D) The level of water will be reasonably stable.
5 (E) Final grading will provide adequate safety and access for
6 proposed water users.
7 (F) The water impoundments will not result in the diminution
8 of the quality or quantity of water used by adjacent or
9 surrounding landowners for agricultural, industrial,
10 recreational, or domestic uses.
11 (12) Conduct an augering operation associated with surface coal
12 mining in a manner that maximizes the recoverability of mineral
13 reserves remaining after the surface coal mining and reclamation
14 operation is complete and seal all auger holes with an impervious
15 and noncombustible material to prevent drainage except where
16 the director determines that the resulting impoundment of water
17 in those auger holes may create a hazard to the environment or the
18 public health or safety. The director may prohibit augering if
19 necessary to:
20 (A) maximize the use, recoverability, or conservation of the
21 solid fuel resources; or
22 (B) protect against adverse water quality impacts.
23 (13) Minimize disturbances to the prevailing hydrologic balance
24 at the mine site and associated offsite areas and to the quality and
25 quantity of water in surface and ground water systems during and
26 after surface coal mining and reclamation operations by doing the
27 following:
28 (A) Avoiding acid or other toxic mine drainage by measures
29 such as the following:
30 (i) Preventing or removing water from contact with
31 toxic-producing deposits.
32 (ii) Treating drainage to reduce toxic content that adversely
33 affects downstream water upon being released to
34 watercourses.
35 (iii) Casing, sealing, or otherwise managing boreholes,
36 shafts, and wells and keep acid or other toxic drainage from
37 entering ground and surface water.
38 (B) Conducting surface coal mining and reclamation
39 operations so as to prevent, to the extent possible using the
40 best technology currently available, violations of the effluent
41 limitations for coal mining operations established under
42 applicable state or federal law.
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

127
1 (C) Constructing siltation structures under clause (B) before
2 commencement of surface coal mining operations that will be
3 certified by an engineer licensed under IC 25-31 IC 25-4-3
4 and constructed as designed and approved in the reclamation
5 plan.
6 (D) Cleaning out and removing temporary or large settling
7 ponds or other siltation structures from drainageways after
8 disturbed areas are revegetated and stabilized and depositing
9 the silt and debris at a site and in a manner approved by the
10 director.
11 (E) Restoring recharge capacity of the mined area to
12 approximate premining conditions.
13 (F) Avoiding channel deepening or enlargement in operations
14 requiring the discharge of water from mines.
15 (G) Other actions required under the permit.
16 (14) With respect to surface disposal of mine wastes, tailings, coal
17 processing wastes, and other wastes in areas other than the mine
18 workings or excavations, the following:
19 (A) Stabilize all waste piles in designated areas through
20 construction in compacted layers, including the use of
21 incombustible and impervious materials if necessary.
22 (B) Assure the following:
23 (i) The final contour of the waste pile will be compatible
24 with natural surroundings.
25 (ii) The site will be stabilized and revegetated according to
26 this article.
27 (15) Refrain from surface coal mining within five hundred (500)
28 feet of active and abandoned underground mines to prevent
29 breakthroughs and to protect the health or safety of miners.
30 However, the director shall permit an operator to mine near,
31 through, or partially through an abandoned underground mine or
32 closer to an active underground mine if the following conditions
33 exist:
34 (A) The nature, timing, and sequencing of the approximate
35 coincidence of specific coal surface mining activities with
36 specific underground coal mining activities are jointly
37 approved by the regulatory authorities concerned with surface
38 coal mining regulation and the health and safety of
39 underground miners.
40 (B) The operations will result in:
41 (i) improved resource recovery;
42 (ii) abatement of water pollution; or
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

128
1 (iii) elimination of hazards to the health and safety of the
2 public.
3 (16) Design, locate, construct, operate, maintain, enlarge, modify,
4 and remove or abandon, in accordance with the standards and
5 criteria used by the United States Secretary of the Interior to
6 ensure that flood control structures are safe and effectively
7 perform their functions, all existing and new coal mine waste
8 piles:
9 (A) consisting of:
10 (i) mine wastes;
11 (ii) tailings;
12 (iii) coal processing wastes; or
13 (iv) other liquid and solid wastes; and
14 (B) used temporarily or permanently as dams or embankments.
15 (17) Ensure the following:
16 (A) All debris, acid-forming materials, toxic materials, or
17 materials constituting a fire hazard are treated, buried, and
18 compacted or otherwise disposed of in a manner designed to
19 prevent contamination of ground or surface water.
20 (B) Contingency plans are developed to prevent sustained
21 combustion.
22 (18) Ensure that explosives are used only in accordance with the
23 following:
24 (A) IC 14-34-12.
25 (B) Applicable state and federal law.
26 (C) The rules adopted by the commission.
27 (19) Ensure that all reclamation efforts proceed in an
28 environmentally sound manner and as contemporaneously as
29 practicable with the surface coal mining operations. However, if
30 the applicant proposes to combine surface coal mining operations
31 with underground coal mining operations to assure maximum
32 practical recovery of the mineral resources, the director may grant
33 a variance for specific areas within the reclamation plan from the
34 requirement that reclamation efforts proceed as
35 contemporaneously as practicable and permit underground coal
36 mining operations before reclamation if the following conditions
37 are met:
38 (A) The director finds in writing the following:
39 (i) The applicant has presented, as part of the permit
40 application, specific, feasible plans for the proposed
41 underground mining operations.
42 (ii) The proposed underground mining operations are
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

129
1 necessary or desirable to assure maximum practical recovery
2 of the mineral resource and will avoid multiple disturbance
3 of the surface.
4 (iii) The applicant has satisfactorily demonstrated that the
5 plan for the underground coal mining operations conforms
6 to the requirements for underground coal mining in that
7 jurisdiction and that permits necessary for the underground
8 coal mining operations have been issued by the appropriate
9 authority.
10 (iv) The applicant has shown the areas proposed for the
11 variance are necessary for the implementation of the
12 proposed underground coal mining operations.
13 (v) Substantial adverse environmental damage, either onsite
14 or offsite, will not result from the delay in completion of
15 reclamation as required by this article.
16 (vi) The provisions for the offsite storage of spoil will
17 comply with subdivision (25).
18 (B) The commission has adopted specific rules to govern the
19 granting of variances in accordance with this subdivision.
20 (C) Variances granted under this subdivision are to be
21 reviewed by the director not more than three (3) years from the
22 date of issuance of the permit.
23 (D) Liability under the bond filed by the applicant with the
24 director under IC 14-34-6 is for the duration of underground
25 coal mining operations and until the requirements of this
26 section and IC 14-34-6 are fully complied with.
27 (20) Ensure that the construction, maintenance, and postmining
28 conditions of access roads into and across the site of operations
29 will control or prevent the following:
30 (A) Erosion and siltation.
31 (B) Pollution of water.
32 (C) Damage to the following:
33 (i) Fish or wildlife or their habitat.
34 (ii) Public or private property.
35 (21) Refrain from the construction of roads or other access ways:
36 (A) up a stream bed or drainage channel; or
37 (B) in the proximity of a channel;
38 that seriously alters the normal flow of water.
39 (22) Establish on the regraded areas and all other land affected a
40 diverse, an effective, and a permanent vegetative cover:
41 (A) of the same seasonal variety native to the area of land to be
42 affected; and
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

130
1 (B) that is capable of self-regeneration and plant succession at
2 least equal in extent of cover to the natural vegetation of the
3 area.
4 However, an introduced species may be used in the revegetation
5 process where desirable and necessary to achieve the approved
6 postmining land use plan.
7 (23) Assume the responsibility for successful revegetation, as
8 required by subdivision (22), as follows:
9 (A) On lands not eligible for remining, for five (5) full years
10 after the last year of augmented seeding, fertilizing, irrigation,
11 or other work to assure compliance with subdivision (22).
12 However, if the director approves a long term intensive
13 agricultural postmining land use, the applicable five (5) or ten
14 (10) year period of responsibility for revegetation commences
15 at the date of initial planting for the long term intensive
16 agricultural postmining land use. If the director issues a
17 written finding approving a long term intensive agricultural
18 postmining land use as part of the mining and reclamation
19 plan, the director may grant exception to subdivision (22).
20 (B) On lands eligible for remining, for two (2) full years after
21 the last year of augmented seeding, fertilizing, irrigation, or
22 other work in order to ensure compliance with subdivision
23 (22).
24 (24) Protect offsite areas from slides or damage occurring during
25 the surface coal mining and reclamation operations and not
26 deposit spoil material or locate any part of the operations or waste
27 accumulations outside the permit area.
28 (25) Place all excess spoil material resulting from coal surface
29 mining and reclamation activities to ensure the following:
30 (A) Spoil is transported and placed in a controlled manner in
31 a position for concurrent compaction and in a manner that
32 assures mass stability and prevents mass movement.
33 (B) The areas of disposal are within the bonded permit areas
34 and all organic matter is removed immediately before spoil
35 placement.
36 (C) Appropriate surface and internal drainage systems and
37 diversion ditches are used in a manner that prevents spoil
38 erosion and movement.
39 (D) The disposal area does not contain springs, natural
40 watercourses, or wet weather seeps unless lateral drains are
41 constructed from the wet areas to the main underdrains in a
42 manner that prevents filtration of the water into the spoil pile.
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

131
1 (E) If placed on a slope, the spoil is placed as follows:
2 (i) On the most moderate slope among the slopes on which,
3 in the judgment of the director, the spoil could be placed in
4 compliance with all the requirements of this article.
5 (ii) If possible, upon or above a natural terrace, bench, or
6 berm if the placement provides additional stability and
7 prevents mass movement.
8 (F) Where the toe of the spoil rests on a downslope, a rock toe
9 buttress of sufficient size to prevent mass movement is
10 constructed.
11 (G) The final configuration is compatible with the natural
12 drainage pattern and surroundings and suitable for intended
13 uses.
14 (H) Design of the spoil disposal area is certified by an
15 engineer licensed under IC 25-31 IC 25-4-3 and in
16 conformance with professional standards.
17 (I) All other provisions of this article are met.
18 (26) To the extent possible using the best technology currently
19 available the following:
20 (A) Minimize disturbances and adverse impacts of the
21 operation on fish, wildlife, and related environmental values.
22 (B) Enhance those resources where practicable.
23 (27) Provide for an undisturbed natural barrier:
24 (A) beginning at the elevation of the lowest coal seam to be
25 mined; and
26 (B) extending from the outslope for a distance determined by
27 the director;
28 to serve as a barrier to slides and erosion.
29 (28) Replace the water supply of an owner of interest in real
30 property who obtains all or part of the owner's supply of water for
31 domestic, agricultural, industrial, or other legitimate use from an
32 underground or a surface source if the supply is affected by
33 contamination, diminution, or interruption proximately resulting
34 from the surface coal mine operation. This article does not affect
35 the right of a person to enforce or protect under applicable law the
36 person's interest in water resources affected by a surface coal
37 mining operation.
38 (29) Meet other criteria that are necessary to achieve reclamation
39 in accordance with the purposes of this article, taking into
40 consideration the physical, climatological, and other
41 characteristics of the site.

34 invasive species in Indiana.

1 (5) Coordinate invasive species education and outreach

31 Sec. 7. (a) The commission on invasive species and pests'

40 money in the fund.
41 (d) The state treasurer shall invest the money in the fund not
42 currently needed to meet the obligations of the fund in the same
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

140
1 manner as other public funds may be invested. Interest that
2 accrues from these investments must be deposited in the fund.
3 (e) Money in the fund may be used for purposes set forth in this
4 chapter and to meet the expenses of administering this chapter.
5 (f) Money in the fund at the end of a state fiscal year does not
6 revert to the state general fund.

6 SECTION 252. IC 15-16-5-64, AS AMENDED BY P.L.29-2024,
7 SECTION 53, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
8 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 64. (a) A person may not produce, transport, store,
9 handle, or dispose of any pesticide product or pesticide product
10 containers in a manner that may:
11 (1) cause injury to:
12 (A) humans;
13 (B) beneficial vegetation;
14 (C) crops;
15 (D) livestock;
16 (E) wildlife; or
17 (F) beneficial insects; or
18 (2) pollute any waterway in a way harmful to any wildlife in a
19 waterway.
20 (b) The board commission may adopt rules governing the
21 production, transportation, storage, handling, and disposal of pesticide
22 products or pesticide containers. In determining these standards, the
23 board commission shall take into consideration any regulations issued
24 by the United States Environmental Protection Agency.
25 (c) Except for pesticides labeled for use in the treatment of drinking
26 water, pesticide storage, mixing, and loading is prohibited within the
27 isolation area of a wellhead protection area zone. Application of
28 pesticides within the isolation area is permitted unless prohibited by the
29 pesticide label.
30 (d) A pesticide storage container located within the wellhead
31 protection area zone and outside of the isolation area must be stored:
32 (1) on an impervious surface designed to contain a discharge of
33 the entire contents of the largest storage container plus the volume
34 displaced by all the other items within the contained area; and
35 (2) in an area protected from precipitation.
36 (e) A pesticide spill or discharge within the wellhead protection area
37 zone must be cleaned up immediately upon discovery and in a manner
38 that prevents the pesticide from threatening waters of the state, dry
39 wells, storm sewers, sanitary sewers, or septic systems.
40 (f) A person may not open burn a pesticide container in a manner
41 that allows air contaminants resulting from combustion to be emitted
42 directly into the air without passing through a stack or chimney from

156
1 an enclosed chamber, as allowed under 326 IAC 4-1.

HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

161
1 under subsection (a) to the state chemist not later than seven (7) days

5 commission a transcript of any record related to the petition, including
6 a transcript of any evidence received.

10 representative shall conduct the hearing in the manner provided by

13 aside, or modify the action of the state chemist. However, the state
14 chemist's finding of facts that are supported by the substantial evidence
15 is considered conclusive.

18 SECTION 256. IC 15-16-5-71, AS ADDED BY P.L.2-2008,
19 SECTION 7, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
20 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 71. (a) A political subdivision (as defined in
21 IC 36-1-2-13) does not have authority to regulate by ordinance the use
22 or application of pesticides.
23 (b) A political subdivision may, by resolution, petition the board
24 commission for a hearing to allow a variance from a rule of the board
25 commission because of special circumstances relating to the use or
26 application of a pesticide. If a petition is received, the board
27 commission shall hold a public hearing to consider allowing the
28 variance requested. The public hearing shall be conducted in an
29 informal manner. IC 4-21.5 does not apply to the public hearing under
30 this section.
31 (c) The board commission may grant a variance requested under
32 this section with or without changes.

2 SECTION 260. IC 15-18-2-9, AS AMENDED BY P.L.186-2014,
3 SECTION 12, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
4 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 9. (a) The creamery examining board is
5 established. The board consists of eight (8) members composed of the
6 following:
7 (1) Three (3) producers of milk and cream.
8 (2) Three (3) processors of milk and cream.
9 (3) One (1) member of the animal sciences department who shall
10 be appointed by the dean of agriculture of Purdue University.
11 (4) One (1) milk hauler.
12 The board shall elect a chairperson annually.
13 (b) Two (2) of the producer members shall be elected to the board
14 by the board of directors of the Indiana Dairy Producers, Inc. The third
15 producer member shall be elected to the board by the Indiana State
16 Dairy Association. If an organization is unable to elect a producer to
17 the board, then the other organization shall elect the producer to the
18 board. However, if both organizations are unable to elect a producer to
19 the board, the dean of agriculture of Purdue University shall appoint a
20 producer to fill the open position after consulting with dairy producers
21 in Indiana.
22 (c) The three (3) processor members shall be elected to the board by
23 the Midwest Dairy Products Association. If the Midwest Dairy
24 Products Association is not able to elect a processor to the board, the
25 dean of agriculture of Purdue University shall appoint a processor to
26 fill the open position after consulting with dairy processors in Indiana.
27 (d) The Midwest Dairy Products Association shall elect one (1)
28 member each year. The board of directors of the Indiana Dairy
29 Producers, Inc., shall elect a producer member to the board two (2) out
30 of every three (3) years and the Indiana State Dairy Association shall
31 select a producer member during the third year.
32 (e) The milk hauler member shall be appointed to the board by the
33 dean of agriculture of Purdue University.
34 (f) This section expires December 31, 2026.
