15 SECTION 264. IC 15-18-2-14, AS ADDED BY P.L.2-2008,
16 SECTION 9, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
17 JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 14. (a) Milk and cream must be purchased
18 on the basis of weight or volume and:
19 (1) the butterfat content;
20 (2) the protein content; or
21 (3) a combination of butterfat and protein content;
22 as determined by any test or tests approved by the board, which may
23 also have been designated official by AOAC International.
24 (b) A payment for milk must be made either on the basis of:
25 (1) a fresh sample taken and tested according to procedures
26 established under rules adopted by the board; or
27 (2) a composite test.
28 A composite sample must cover a period of not more than sixteen (16)
29 days. In each plant, all composite samples must cover the same period
30 for each test and succeeding periods must be as nearly equal as
31 possible. All composite samples must cover the same period. A
32 uniform sample consisting of at least ten (10) cubic centimeters of milk
33 must be taken each day, except when milk is frozen or partly churned,
34 from the milk furnished by each person. An approved preservative
35 must be added to the sample and each composite sample must be held
36 in an approved, rubber stoppered, airtight bottle that is plainly labeled
37 to show the name or number of the person whose milk the composite
38 sample represents. The milk to be sampled must be poured into a weigh
39 tank approved by the creamery license division board meeting the
40 standards prescribed in rules, be run through a fine mesh or perforated
41 metal strainer, and be homogeneous in richness before any sample is
42 taken. The composite samples must be kept in a refrigerated cabinet or
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

164
1 cooler in or near the receiving room at a temperature of not more than
2 forty-two (42) degrees Fahrenheit, away from strong light, and must be
3 kept free of mold. The test must be made within seventy-two (72)
4 hours, excluding Sundays and holidays, after the last sample has been
5 added to the composite sample.
6 (c) If at the close of any composite period the composite samples are
7 inaccurate as determined by the creamery license division, board, three
8 (3) daily samples must be taken within the first five (5) days of the
9 following composite period. The average of the three (3) fresh samples
10 determines the basis of payment for the composite period in question.
11 (d) In each plant where composite samples of milk are taken, the
12 owner or manager shall delegate an individual who is responsible for
13 the care, condition, and accuracy of all composite samples.
14 (e) After the composite samples are emptied, the sample bottles
15 must be thoroughly washed, and all bottles and rubber stoppers shall
16 be sterilized before being used again.
17 (f) Composite samples may be removed from the plant for testing
18 elsewhere only on written permission from the creamery license
19 division. board.

8 SECTION 285. IC 16-19-3-27.5, AS AMENDED BY P.L.151-2025,
9 SECTION 1, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
10 JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 27.5. (a) As used in this section and section
11 27.8 of this chapter, "technology new to Indiana" (referred to in this
12 section as "TNI") means sewage treatment or disposal methods,
13 processes, or equipment that are not described in the administrative
14 rules of the state department or the executive board concerning
15 residential onsite sewage systems (410 IAC 6-8.3) or commercial
16 onsite sewage systems (410 IAC 6-10.1).
17 (b) The state department shall establish and maintain a technical
18 review panel consisting of individuals with technical or scientific
19 knowledge relating to onsite sewage systems. The technical review
20 panel:
21 (1) shall decide under subsection (f) whether to approve:
22 (A) proprietary residential wastewater treatment devices; and
23 (B) proprietary commercial wastewater treatment devices;
24 for general use in Indiana;
25 (2) shall biannually review the performance of residential onsite
26 sewage systems and commercial onsite sewage systems;
27 (3) shall assist the state department in developing standards and
28 guidelines for proprietary residential wastewater treatment
29 devices and proprietary commercial wastewater treatment
30 devices;
31 (4) shall assist the executive board and the state department in
32 updating rules adopted under section 4 of this chapter concerning
33 residential onsite sewage systems and commercial onsite sewage
34 systems; and
35 (5) may exercise the powers granted by section 27.7 of this
36 chapter.
37 (c) The technical review panel shall include the following:
38 (1) A member of the staff of the state department, who shall serve
39 as the chair.
40 (2) A local health department environmental health specialist
41 appointed by the governor.
42 (3) An Indiana professional engineer registered under IC 25-31-1
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

173
1 IC 25-4-3 representing the American Council of Engineering
2 Companies.
3 (4) A representative of the Indiana Builders Association.

6 Scientists.

8 specialty in engineering, soil science, environmental health, or
9 biology appointed by the governor.
10 (7) A representative of the Indiana Onsite Wastewater
11 Professionals Association.
12 (8) An Indiana onsite sewage system contractor appointed by the
13 governor.
14 (9) A representative of the Indiana State Building and
15 Construction Trades Council.
16 All members of the technical review panel are voting members, except
17 the member described in subdivision (1).
18 (d) In the case of a tie vote of the technical review panel, the
19 following shall occur:
20 (1) If the tie vote is in regard to a county, city, or town ordinance
21 concerning residential onsite sewage systems:
22 (A) the technical review panel shall send the ordinance back
23 to the county, city, or town not more than seven (7) days after
24 the day of the tie vote;
25 (B) the county, city, or town may revise and resubmit the
26 ordinance; and
27 (C) if the ordinance is revised and resubmitted, the technical
28 review panel shall consider the revised ordinance at its next
29 meeting.
30 (2) If the tie vote is in regard to a matter not described in
31 subdivision (1), the technical review panel shall, not more than
32 seven (7) days after the day of the tie vote:
33 (A) contact the applicant by phone call and by mail; and
34 (B) request more information or provide an explanation of how
35 the applicant can modify the application to make it more
36 complete.
37 The technical review panel shall review any new information
38 provided by the applicant and vote again on the application not
39 more than thirty (30) days after receiving the information.
40 (e) The technical review panel shall do the following:
41 (1) Receive applications for the approval of TNI for general use
42 in:
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

174
1 (A) residential onsite sewage systems under sections 4 and 27
2 of this chapter and IC 16-41-25; and
3 (B) commercial onsite sewage systems under sections 4 and 27
4 of this chapter and IC 16-19-3.5.
5 (2) Meet monthly as needed to assist the executive board and the
6 state department as described in subsection (b)(4) and to review
7 applications described in subdivision (1).

18 SECTION 286. IC 16-19-3-27.6, AS ADDED BY P.L.167-2022,
19 SECTION 5, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
20 JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 27.6. (a) As used in this section, "type of
21 residential onsite sewage system" means an onsite sewage system that:
22 (1) has a particular design;
23 (2) consists of particular components; and
24 (3) is installed or constructed in a particular way.
25 (b) For purposes of this section, the following are different types of
26 residential onsite sewage systems:
27 (1) A conventional septic system consisting of a septic tank and
28 a gravel or stone soil absorption field.
29 (2) A chamber system in which wastewater is piped from the
30 septic tank to a series of connected chambers backfilled with soil.
31 (3) A subsurface drip distribution system in which wastewater is
32 pumped from the septic tank to a dose tank and then gradually
33 delivered to drip laterals in the soil.
34 (4) An aerobic treatment unit in which oxygen is injected into the
35 wastewater to accelerate the biological processes used to treat the
36 wastewater before being discharged to a soil absorption field.
37 (5) A recirculating sand filter system in which wastewater flows
38 from a septic tank to a pump chamber and then is recirculated
39 through a sand filter before being discharged to a soil absorption
40 field.
41 (6) An evapotranspiration system that includes a drain field
42 having a watertight liner and in which wastewater, after entering
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

177
1 the drain field, evaporates into the air.
2 (7) A constructed wetland system in which wastewater from a
3 septic tank enters a wetland cell containing an impermeable liner,
4 gravel, sand fill, and wetland plants that remove pathogens and
5 nutrients from the wastewater before being discharged to a soil
6 absorption field.
7 (8) A sand-lined system in which wastewater flows from a septic
8 tank to a combined treatment and dispersal soil absorption field
9 containing piping laterals surrounded by sand.
10 (c) If:
11 (1) the local health department of a county, city, or multiple
12 county unit has issued a permit for construction or installation of
13 a type of residential onsite sewage system;
14 (2) the use of the same type of residential onsite sewage system
15 at a location in a different county, city, or multiple county unit has
16 been approved by:
17 (A) a professional engineer registered under IC 25-31-1;
18 IC 25-4-3;
19 (B) a registered soil scientist (as defined in IC 25-31.5-1-7);
20 (C) an individual or entity engaged in the business of
21 constructing and installing residential onsite sewage systems;
22 and
23 (D) the designer of the system, if the system was designed by
24 someone other than a person referred to in clause (A) or (C);
25 and
26 (3) all of the persons referred to in subdivision (2) attest that the
27 same type of residential onsite sewage system, if constructed or
28 installed at the location referred to in subdivision (2), would
29 satisfy the requirements of the rule of the state department
30 concerning residential onsite sewage systems;
31 the local health department of the county, city, or multiple county unit
32 referred to in subdivision (2) may not refuse to issue a permit for the
33 construction or installation of a residential onsite sewage system of the
34 type referred to in subdivisions (1), (2), and (3) at the location referred
35 to in subdivision (2).

4 purposes:
5 (1) To educate and advocate for women's health by requesting that

13 and concerns relating to the reproductive, menopausal, and
14 postmenopausal phases of a woman's life, with an emphasis on
15 postmenopausal health.

17 regarding women's health data, strategies, services, and programs
18 that address women's health issues, including the following:
19 (A) Diseases that significantly impact women, including heart
20 disease, cancer, and osteoporosis.

22 (C) Mental health.
23 (D) Substance abuse.
24 (E) Sexually transmitted diseases.
25 (F) Sexual assault and domestic violence.
26 (G) Female genital mutilation (as defined in IC 35-42-2-10).

28 and data conducted or compiled by:

35 following:

37 (B) Diseases that significantly affect women, including heart

10 malfunction of the residential onsite sewage system that creates a clear
11 and immediate danger to the public's health, safety, or property.
12 (e) This section shall not be interpreted as prohibiting or restricting
13 the periodic:
14 (1) inspection;
15 (2) servicing;
16 (3) testing; or
17 (4) maintenance;
18 of an onsite residential sewage discharging disposal system (as defined
19 in IC 13-11-2-144.7).
20 SECTION 302. IC 16-41-25-11, AS ADDED BY P.L.232-2023,
21 SECTION 13, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
22 JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 11. (a) As used in this section, "residential
23 onsite sewage system failure" means a residential onsite sewage system
24 that exhibits one (1) or more of the following:
25 (1) The onsite sewage system refuses to accept sewage at the rate
26 of design application and interferes with the normal use of
27 residential plumbing fixtures.
28 (2) Effluent discharge exceeds the absorptive capacity of the soil,
29 resulting in ponding, seepage, or other discharge of the effluent
30 to the ground surface or to surface waters.
31 (3) Effluent is discharged from the onsite sewage system causing
32 contamination of a potable water supply, ground water, or surface
33 waters.
34 (b) A local health department that determines that a residential
35 onsite sewage system is in residential onsite sewage system failure may
36 issue an order requiring that the failure be corrected. However, an order
37 issued under this subsection is stayed if the owner or occupant of the
38 residence served by the residential onsite sewage system, within
39 fourteen (14) days after the date of the order, enters into a contract
40 with:
41 (1) a septic system installer or inspector registered with the
42 county in which the residential onsite sewage system is located;
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

190
1 (2) an onsite sewage system technician; or
2 (3) an Indiana professional engineer registered under IC 25-31-1;
3 IC 25-4-3;
4 under which the installer or inspector, onsite sewage system technician,
5 or professional engineer agrees to conduct an inspection to determine
6 whether the residential onsite sewage system is in residential onsite
7 sewage system failure.
8 (c) An order is stayed under subsection (b) until the earlier of the
9 following:
10 (1) The date on which the installer or inspector, technician, or
11 engineer performs the inspection and presents the results of the
12 inspection in writing to:
13 (A) the owner or occupant of the residence served by the
14 residential onsite sewage system; and
15 (B) the local health department.
16 (2) The expiration of forty-five (45) days after the date of the
17 order issued under subsection (b).
18 (d) If the installer or inspector, onsite sewage system technician, or
19 professional engineer states in the report provided to the local health
20 department that the residential onsite sewage system is not in
21 residential onsite sewage system failure, the local health department
22 shall:
23 (1) withdraw the order issued under subsection (b); or
24 (2) conduct an investigation of the residential onsite sewage
25 system to make a new determination whether the residential
26 onsite sewage system is in residential onsite sewage system
27 failure.
28 (e) If the local health department conducts an investigation under
29 subsection (d)(2), the order issued under subsection (b) is stayed
30 pending the new determination of the local health department based on
31 the investigation.

14 registry for traumatic spinal cord and brain injuries.

28 services at affordable rates to an individual with a spinal cord

31 (10%) and not more than fifteen percent (15%) of money in the
32 fund for:
33 (A) post acute extended treatment and services for an
34 individual with a brain injury; or
35 (B) facilities that offer long term activity based therapy
36 services at affordable rates to an individual with a brain injury
37 that requires extended post acute care.
38 (6) (5) Develop a statewide trauma system. However, not more
39 than fifty percent (50%) of money in the fund may be used for
40 purposes of developing a statewide trauma system.

HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

205
1 (C) Requirements for notifying parents.
2 (D) Training regarding the use of restraint and seclusion,
3 including the frequency of training and what employees must
4 be trained.
5 (E) The distribution of the seclusion and restraint policy to
6 parents and the public.
7 (F) Requirements for the reporting of incidents of restraint and
8 seclusion in the annual school performance report, including
9 incidents of restraint and seclusion involving school resource
10 officers (as defined in IC 20-26-18.2-1).
11 (G) Circumstances that may require more timely incident
12 reporting and the requirements for such reporting.
13 (2) To develop, maintain, and revise a model restraint and
14 seclusion plan for schools that includes the following elements:
15 (A) A statement on how students will be treated with dignity
16 and respect and how appropriate student behavior will be
17 promoted and taught.
18 (B) A statement ensuring that the school will use prevention,
19 positive behavior intervention and support, and conflict
20 de-escalation to eliminate or minimize the need for use of any
21 of the following:
22 (i) Seclusion.
23 (ii) Chemical restraint.
24 (iii) Mechanical restraint.
25 (iv) Physical restraint.
26 (v) Time-out.
27 (C) A statement ensuring that any behavioral intervention used
28 will be consistent with the student's most current behavioral
29 intervention plan, or individualized education program, if
30 applicable.
31 (D) Definitions for restraint and seclusion, as defined in this
32 chapter.
33 (E) A statement ensuring that if a procedure listed in clause
34 (B) is used, the procedure will be used:
35 (i) as a last resort safety procedure, employed only after
36 another, less restrictive procedure has been implemented
37 without success; and
38 (ii) in a situation in which there is an imminent risk of injury
39 to the student, other students, school employees, or visitors
40 to the school.
41 (F) An indication that restraint or seclusion may be used only
42 for a short time period, or until the imminent risk of injury has
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

206
1 passed.
2 (G) A documentation and recording requirement governing
3 instances in which procedures listed in clause (B) are used,
4 including:
5 (i) how every incident will be documented and debriefed;
6 (ii) how responsibilities will be assigned to designated
7 employees for evaluation and oversight; and
8 (iii) designation of a school employee to be the keeper of
9 such documents.
10 (H) A requirement that the student's parent must be notified as
11 soon as possible when an incident involving the student occurs
12 that includes use of procedures listed in clause (B).
13 (I) A requirement that a copy of an incident report must be
14 sent to the student's parent after the student is subject to a
15 procedure listed in clause (B).
16 (J) Required recurrent training for appropriate school
17 employees on the appropriate use of effective alternatives to

19 behavioral intervention and support and conflict de-escalation.

2 SECTION 353. IC 21-18.5-2-12, AS AMENDED BY P.L.273-2013,
3 SECTION 8, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
4 JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 12. (a) "Postsecondary credit bearing
5 proprietary educational institution" means a degree granting and credit
6 bearing institution that provides instructional or educational services
7 or training, whether onsite, online, or through any combination of these
8 or other instructional modalities, and is accredited by an accrediting
9 agency recognized by the United States Department of Education or is
10 seeking and progressing toward accreditation by an accrediting agency
11 recognized by the United States Department of Education.
12 (b) The term does not include the following:
13 (1) An Indiana state educational institution or another Indiana
14 educational institution established by law and financed in whole
15 or in part by public funds.
16 (2) A postsecondary proprietary educational institution approved
17 or regulated by any other state regulatory board, agency, or
18 commission other than the board for proprietary education.
19 commission for higher education.
20 (3) An elementary or secondary school attended by students in
21 kindergarten or grades 1 through 12 and supported in whole or in
22 part by private tuition payments.
23 (4) Any educational institution or educational training that:
24 (A) is maintained or given by an employer or a group of
25 employers, without charge, for employees or for individuals
26 the employer anticipates employing;
27 (B) is maintained or given by a labor organization, without
28 charge, for its members or apprentices;
29 (C) offers exclusively instruction that is clearly
30 self-improvement, motivational, or avocational in intent
31 (including instruction in dance, music, or self-defense, and
32 private tutoring); or
33 (D) is a Montessori or nursery school.
34 (5) A privately endowed two (2) or four (4) year degree granting
35 institution that is regionally accredited and whose principal
36 campus is located in Indiana.
37 (6) Out-of-state public and nonprofit degree granting institutions
38 offering instructional or educational services or training in
39 Indiana.
40 (7) A religious institution that offers educational instruction or an
41 educational program of a clearly religious nature.

7 SECTION 356. IC 21-18.5-5-2 IS REPEALED [EFFECTIVE
8 JANUARY 1, 2027]. Sec. 2. (a) The board for proprietary education
9 consists of the following seven (7) members:
10 (1) The secretary of education or the secretary's designee.
11 (2) The executive officer of the commission for higher education
12 or the executive officer's designee.
13 (3) Five (5) members appointed by the governor.
14 (b) The members appointed by the governor under subsection (a)
15 serve for a term of four (4) years.
16 (c) Not more than three (3) of the members appointed by the
17 governor may be members of the same political party.
18 (d) Of the five (5) members appointed by the governor:
19 (1) one (1) must have been engaged for a period of at least five
20 (5) years immediately preceding appointment in an executive or
21 a managerial position in a postsecondary proprietary educational
22 institution subject to IC 21-18.5-6;
23 (2) one (1) must have been engaged in administering or managing
24 an industrial employee training program for a period of at least
25 five (5) years immediately preceding appointment; and
26 (3) three (3) must be representatives of the public at large who are
27 not representatives of the types of postsecondary credit bearing
28 proprietary educational institutions to be authorized.
29 For purposes of subdivision (3), an elected or appointed state or local
30 official or a member of a private or public school may not be appointed
31 as a representative of the public at large.
32 (e) An appointment to fill a vacancy occurring on the board for
33 proprietary education is for the unexpired term.

27 SECTION 15, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
28 JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 6. (a) The career college student assurance
29 fund is established to provide indemnification to a student or an
30 enrollee of a postsecondary credit bearing proprietary educational
31 institution who suffers loss or damage as a result of:
32 (1) the failure or neglect of the postsecondary credit bearing
33 proprietary educational institution to faithfully perform all
34 agreements, express or otherwise, with the student, enrollee, one
35 (1) or both of the parents of the student or enrollee, or a guardian
36 of the student or enrollee as represented by the application for the
37 institution's authorization and the materials submitted in support
38 of that application;
39 (2) the failure or neglect of the postsecondary credit bearing
40 proprietary educational institution to maintain and operate a
41 course or courses of instruction or study in compliance with the
42 standards of this chapter; or
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

224
1 (3) an agent's misrepresentation in procuring the student's
2 enrollment.
3 (b) The board for proprietary education commission for higher
4 education shall administer the fund.
5 (c) The expenses of administering the fund shall be paid from
6 money in the fund.
7 (d) The treasurer of state shall invest the money in the fund not
8 currently needed to meet the obligations of the fund in the same
9 manner as other public funds may be invested.
10 (e) Money in the fund at the end of a state fiscal year does not revert
11 to the state general fund but remains available to be used for providing
12 money for reimbursements allowed under this chapter.
13 (f) Upon the fund acquiring fifty thousand dollars ($50,000), the
14 balance in the fund must not become less than fifty thousand dollars
15 ($50,000). If:
16 (1) a claim against the fund is filed that would, if paid in full,
17 require the balance of the fund to become less than fifty thousand
18 dollars ($50,000); and
19 (2) the board for proprietary education commission for higher
20 education determines that the student is eligible for a
21 reimbursement under the fund;
22 the board for proprietary education commission for higher education
23 shall prorate the amount of the reimbursement to ensure that the
24 balance of the fund does not become less than fifty thousand dollars
25 ($50,000), and the student is entitled to receive that balance of the
26 student's claim from the fund as money becomes available in the fund
27 from contributions to the fund required under this chapter.
28 (g) The board for proprietary education commission for higher
29 education shall ensure that all outstanding claim amounts described in
30 subsection (f) are paid as money in the fund becomes available in the
31 chronological order of the outstanding claims.
32 (h) A claim against the fund may not be construed to be a debt of the
33 state.
34 SECTION 365. IC 21-18.5-6-7, AS ADDED BY P.L.107-2012,
35 SECTION 58, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
36 JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 7. (a) Except as otherwise provided in this
37 section, each postsecondary credit bearing proprietary educational
38 institution shall make quarterly contributions to the fund. The quarters
39 begin January 1, April 1, July 1, and October 1.
40 (b) For each quarter, each postsecondary credit bearing proprietary
41 educational institution shall make a contribution equal to the STEP
42 THREE amount derived under the following formula:
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

225
1 STEP ONE: Determine the total amount of tuition and fees earned
2 during the quarter.
3 STEP TWO: Multiply the STEP ONE amount by one-tenth of one
4 percent (0.1%).
5 STEP THREE: Add the STEP TWO amount and sixty dollars
6 ($60).
7 (c) After June 30, 2012, upon the career college student assurance
8 fund achieving at least an initial balance of five hundred thousand
9 dollars ($500,000), a postsecondary credit bearing proprietary
10 educational institution that contributes to the career college student
11 assurance fund when the initial quarterly contribution is required under
12 this chapter after the fund's establishment is not required to make
13 contributions to the fund.
14 (d) The board for proprietary education commission for higher
15 education shall determine the number of quarterly contributions
16 required for the career college student assurance fund to initially
17 accumulate five hundred thousand dollars ($500,000).
18 (e) Except as provided in subsections (a), (b), and (f), a
19 postsecondary credit bearing proprietary educational institution that
20 begins making contributions to the career college student assurance
21 fund after the initial quarterly contribution as required under this
22 section shall make contributions to the fund for the same number of
23 quarters as determined by the board for proprietary education
24 commission for higher education under subsection (d).
25 (f) If, after a career college student assurance fund acquires five
26 hundred thousand dollars ($500,000), the balance in the fund becomes
27 less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars ($250,000), all
28 postsecondary credit bearing proprietary educational institutions not
29 required to make contributions to the career college student assurance
30 fund as described in subsection (c) or (e) shall make contributions to
31 the career college student assurance fund for the number of quarters
32 necessary for the fund to accumulate five hundred thousand dollars
33 ($500,000).

27 SECTION 368. IC 21-18.5-6-11, AS AMENDED BY P.L.273-2013,

29 JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 11. Full authorization under this chapter may
30 not be issued unless and until the board for proprietary education
31 commission for higher education finds that the postsecondary credit
32 bearing proprietary educational institution meets minimum standards
33 that are appropriate to that type or class of postsecondary credit bearing
34 proprietary educational institution, including the following minimum
35 standards:
36 (1) The postsecondary credit bearing proprietary educational
37 institution has a sound financial structure with sufficient
38 resources for continued support.
39 (2) The postsecondary credit bearing proprietary educational
40 institution has satisfactory training or educational facilities with
41 sufficient tools, supplies, or equipment and the necessary number
42 of work stations or classrooms to adequately train, instruct, or
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

227
1 educate the number of students enrolled or proposed to be
2 enrolled.
3 (3) The postsecondary credit bearing proprietary educational
4 institution has an adequate number of qualified instructors or
5 teachers, sufficiently trained by experience or education, to give
6 the instruction, education, or training contemplated.
7 (4) The advertising and representations made on behalf of the
8 postsecondary credit bearing proprietary educational institution
9 to prospective students are truthful and free from
10 misrepresentation or fraud.
11 (5) The charge made for the training, instruction, or education is
12 clearly stated and based upon the services rendered.
13 (6) The premises and conditions under which the students work
14 and study are sanitary, healthful, and safe according to modern
15 standards.
16 (7) The postsecondary credit bearing proprietary educational
17 institution has and follows a refund policy approved by the board
18 for proprietary education. commission for higher education.
19 (8) The owner or chief administrator of the postsecondary credit
20 bearing proprietary educational institution is subject to a
21 background check by the board for proprietary education
22 commission for higher education and has not been convicted of
23 a felony.
24 (9) The owner or chief administrator of the postsecondary credit
25 bearing proprietary educational institution has not been the owner
26 or chief administrator of a postsecondary credit bearing
27 proprietary educational institution that has had its authorization
28 revoked or has been closed involuntarily in the five (5) year
29 period preceding the application for authorization. However, if
30 the owner or chief administrator of the postsecondary credit
31 bearing proprietary educational institution has been the owner or
32 chief administrator of a postsecondary credit bearing proprietary
33 educational institution that has had its authorization revoked or
34 has been closed involuntarily more than five (5) years before the
35 application for authorization, the board for proprietary education
36 commission for higher education may issue full authorization at
37 the board for proprietary education's discretion.

39 behavioral health and addiction treatment development program
40 established under this chapter. The account shall be administered by
41 the division of mental health and addiction. Money in the account shall
42 be used to fund residency positions, fellowship training, and
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

240
1 certification in addiction psychiatry, including:
2 (1) educational expenses;
3 (2) grants and scholarships;
4 (3) salaries; and
5 (4) benefits.

8 (2) grants; and
9 (3) gifts and bequests.
10 (d) The expenses of administering the account shall be paid from
11 money in the account.
12 (e) The treasurer of state shall invest the money in the account not
13 currently needed to meet the obligations of the account in the same
14 manner as other public money may be invested. Interest that accrues
15 from these investments shall be deposited in the account.
16 (f) Money in the account at the end of a state fiscal year does not
17 revert to the state general fund.

19 SECTION 425. IC 22-8-1.1-17.1 IS AMENDED TO READ AS
20 FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 17.1. (a) Any
21 standard promulgated under this chapter shall prescribe the use of
22 labels or other appropriate forms of warning as are necessary to insure
23 that employees are apprised of all hazards to which they are exposed,
24 relevant symptoms and appropriate emergency treatment, and proper
25 conditions and precautions of safe use or exposure. Where appropriate,
26 such a standard shall also prescribe suitable protective equipment and
27 control or technological procedures to be used in connection with the
28 hazards and shall provide for monitoring or measuring employee
29 exposure at such locations and intervals and in such manner as may be
30 necessary for the protection of employees. In addition where
31 appropriate, any standard shall prescribe the type or frequency of
32 medical examinations or other tests which shall be made available by
33 the employer, at employer's cost, to employees exposed to hazards in
34 order to most effectively determine whether the health of the
35 employees is adversely affected by the exposure. Upon request, the
36 results of examinations or tests shall be furnished to the department and
37 shall remain confidential within the department. At the request of the
38 employee, results shall be furnished to his physician.
39 (b) The commission, board, in promulgating standards dealing with
40 toxic materials or harmful physical agents, shall set the standard which
41 most adequately assures, to the extent feasible, on the basis of the best
42 available evidence, that no employee will suffer material impairment
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

252
1 of health or functional capacity even if the employee has regular
2 exposure to the hazard dealt with by the standard for the period of his
3 working life. Development of standards shall be based upon research,
4 demonstrations, experiments, and such other information as may be
5 appropriate. In addition to the attainment of the highest degree of
6 health and safety protection for the employee, other considerations
7 shall be the latest available scientific data in the field, the feasibility of
8 the standards, and experience gained under this and other health and
9 safety laws. Whenever practicable, the standard promulgated shall be
10 expressed in terms of objective criteria and of the performance desired.
11 (c) The commission, board, in promulgating standards, shall adopt
12 rules requiring employers to maintain accurate records of employee
13 exposures to potentially toxic material or harmful physical agents
14 which are required to be monitored or measured under the standards.
15 These rules shall provide employees or their representatives with an
16 opportunity to observe monitoring or measuring and to have access to
17 the records thereof. These rules shall also make appropriate provisions
18 for each employee to have access to such records as will indicate his
19 own exposure to toxic materials or harmful physical agents. Under
20 these rules, each employer shall notify any employee who is being
21 consistently exposed to toxic materials or harmful physical agents in
22 concentrations or at levels which exceed those prescribed by an
23 occupational safety and health standard and shall inform any employee
24 who is being thus exposed of the corrective action being taken.
25 SECTION 426. IC 22-8-1.1-19 IS AMENDED TO READ AS
26 FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 19. Standards:
27 Declaratory Judgment. After promulgation of a safety standard by the
28 commission, board, any question as to its applicability or legal validity
29 may be adjudicated by an action for a declaratory judgment filed by an
30 affected person or firm under IC 34-14-1 (or IC 34-4-10 before its
31 repeal).
32 SECTION 427. IC 22-8-1.1-19.1 IS AMENDED TO READ AS
33 FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 19.1. Temporary
34 Variances. Any employer may apply to the commission board for a
35 temporary order granting a variance from a standard or any provision
36 thereof promulgated under this chapter. Such temporary order shall be
37 granted only if the employer establishes that he is unable to comply
38 with a standard by its effective date because of unavailability of
39 professional or technical personnel or of materials and equipment
40 needed to come into compliance with the standard or because necessary
41 construction or alteration of facilities cannot be completed by the
42 effective date; that he is taking all available steps to safeguard his
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

253
1 employees against the hazards covered by the standard; and that he has
2 an effective program for coming into compliance with a standard as
3 quickly as practicable. Any temporary order issued under this section
4 shall prescribe the practices, means, methods, operations and processes
5 which the employer must adopt and use while the order is in effect and
6 state in detail his program for coming into compliance with the
7 standard. Such a temporary order may be granted only after notice to
8 employees and an opportunity for a hearing. Said notice shall be given
9 to the authorized representative of the employees and be posted at or
10 near the location for which the variance is sought. No order for a
11 temporary variance may be in effect for longer than the period needed
12 by the employer to achieve compliance with the standard or one (1)
13 year, whichever is shorter, except that such an order may be renewed
14 not more than twice, so long as the requirements of this paragraph are
15 met and if an application for renewal is filed at least ninety (90) days
16 prior to the expiration date of the order.
17 SECTION 428. IC 22-8-1.1-20.1 IS AMENDED TO READ AS
18 FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 20.1. Any
19 affected employer may apply to the commission board for a permanent
20 variance from a standard promulgated under this chapter. Affected
21 employees shall be given notice of each such application by posting it
22 at or near the location for which the variance is sought, and an
23 opportunity to participate in a hearing. The commission board shall
24 issue such rule or order if it determines, after a hearing, including an
25 inspection, if appropriate, that the proponent of the variance has
26 demonstrated by a preponderance of the evidence that the conditions,
27 practices, means, methods, operations, or processes used or proposed
28 to be used by the employer will provide employment and places of
29 employment to his employees which are as safe and healthful as those
30 which would prevail if he complied with the standard. The rule or order
31 so issued shall prescribe the conditions the employer must maintain,
32 and the practices, means, methods, operations, and processes which he
33 must adopt and utilize to the extent they differ from the standard in
34 question. Such a rule or order may be modified or revoked upon
35 application by an employer, employees, the commissioner of labor, or
36 the commission board on its own motion, in the manner prescribed for
37 its issuance under this section at any time after six (6) months from its
38 issuance, provided that the moving party gives thirty (30) days notice
39 to the other parties, and a hearing is held at the request of any of the
40 parties.

12 SECTION 434. IC 22-9.5-5-5 IS AMENDED TO READ AS
13 FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 5. (a) A person may not
14 discriminate in the sale or rental or otherwise make unavailable or deny
15 a dwelling to any buyer or renter because of a disability of:
16 (1) the buyer or renter;
17 (2) a person residing in or intending to reside in the dwelling after
18 the dwelling is sold, rented, or made available; or
19 (3) any person associated with the buyer or renter.
20 (b) A person may not discriminate against any person in the terms,
21 conditions, or privileges of sale or rental of a dwelling or in the
22 provision of services or facilities in connection with the dwelling
23 because of a disability of:
24 (1) the person;
25 (2) a person residing in or intending to reside in the dwelling after
26 the dwelling is sold, rented, or made available; or
27 (3) any person associated with the person.
28 (c) For purposes of this section only, discrimination includes the
29 following:
30 (1) A refusal to permit, at the expense of the person with a
31 disability, reasonable modifications of existing premises occupied
32 or to be occupied by the person if the modifications may be
33 necessary to afford the person full enjoyment of the premises.
34 (2) A refusal to make reasonable accommodations in rules,
35 policies, practices, or services, when the accommodations may be
36 necessary to afford the person equal opportunity to use and enjoy
37 a dwelling.
38 (3) In connection with the design and construction of covered
39 multifamily dwellings for first occupancy after March 13, 1991,
40 a failure to design and construct those dwellings in a manner that:
41 (A) the public use and common use parts of the dwellings are
42 readily accessible to and usable by persons with disabilities;
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

256
1 (B) all the doors are designed to allow passage into and within
2 all premises within the dwellings and are sufficiently wide to
3 allow passage by persons with disabilities in wheelchairs; and
4 (C) all premises within the dwellings contain the following
5 features of adaptive design:
6 (i) An accessible route into and through the dwelling.
7 (ii) Light switches, electrical outlets, thermostats, and other
8 environmental controls in accessible locations.
9 (iii) Reinforcements in bathroom walls to allow later
10 installation of grab bars.
11 (iv) Usable kitchens and bathrooms so that an individual in
12 a wheelchair can maneuver about the space.
13 (d) As used in subsection (c), "covered multifamily dwellings"
14 means:
15 (1) buildings consisting of four (4) or more units if the buildings
16 have one (1) or more elevators; and
17 (2) ground floor units in other buildings consisting of four (4) or
18 more units.
19 (e) Compliance with the rules of the fire prevention and building
20 safety commission department of homeland security that incorporate
21 by reference the appropriate requirements of the American National
22 Standard for buildings and facilities providing accessibility and
23 usability for people with physical disabilities (ANSI A117.1) satisfies
24 the requirements of subsection (c)(3)(C).
25 (f) This section does not require that a dwelling be made available
26 to an individual whose tenancy would constitute a direct threat to the
27 health or safety of other individuals or whose tenancy would result in
28 substantial physical damage to the property of others.

3 SECTION 441. IC 22-10-3-1 IS REPEALED [EFFECTIVE
4 JANUARY 1, 2027]. Sec. 1. As used in this article:
5 "Active workings" means all places in a mine that are ventilated and
6 inspected regularly.
7 "Belt examiner" means an individual designated by the mine
8 foreman to perform the functions as required by 30 CFR Part 75 in
9 connection with examinations to ensure that the belt, belt drives, dump
10 points, air movement, roof, and ribs of a mine are in safe condition.
11 "Board" refers to the mining board established under
12 IC 22-10-1.5-2.
13 "Commercial mine" means any underground mine from which coal
14 is produced for sale, exchange, or commercial use.
15 "Director" means the director of the bureau of mines and mine
16 safety established under IC 22-1-1-4.
17 "Hoisting engineer" means an individual who is capable of
18 transporting people and material in and out of a mine by means of a
19 hoist.
20 "Interested persons" means the director, safety personnel designated
21 by the operator, state and federal coal mine inspectors, and, to the
22 extent required by law, any other person.
23 "Mine" means an underground commercial coal mine.
24 "Mine electrician" means a properly certified individual who can
25 perform electrical work in:
26 (1) a surface coal mine;
27 (2) surface areas of underground coal mines; and
28 (3) underground coal mines.
29 "Mine examiner" means a properly certified person designated by
30 the mine foreman to examine the mine for gas and other dangers. A
31 mine examiner may temporarily act as a section foreman if designated
32 to act as such by the mine foreman.
33 "Mine foreman" means the person charged with the responsibility
34 of the general supervision of the underground working of a mine and
35 the persons employed in the mine and for the health and safety of those
36 employees.
37 "Mine inspector" means the person appointed to assist in
38 administering this article.
39 "Mine Safety Administration" refers to the Mine Safety and Health
40 Administration, United States Department of Labor.
41 "Mining laws" means:
42 (1) this article;
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

260
1 (2) IC 22-1-1-5(a);
2 (3) 30 CFR part 75; and
3 (4) 30 CFR part 77.
4 "Operator" means an individual, firm, association, partnership,
5 limited liability company, or corporation operating an underground
6 coal mine or any part of a mine.
7 "Shot-firer" means a properly certified person designated by the
8 mine foreman to perform the functions as required in this article in
9 connection with breaking down coal or rock.

23 SECTION 444. IC 22-10-3-10, AS AMENDED BY P.L.10-2012,
24 SECTION 3, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
25 JANUARY 1, 2027]: Sec. 10. (a) It is the duty of the board bureau to
26 examine any person applying for a certificate for mine foreman,
27 shot-firer, mine examiner, hoisting engineer, mine electrician, or belt
28 examiner and to issue certificates of competency to the applicants who,
29 upon examination, prove themselves competent and qualified.
30 (b) A certificate is valid only when the examination for certification
31 has been held in the presence of a member of the board and signed by
32 the chairman of the board. an employee of the bureau. A certificate
33 of competency may not be issued to any person whose grade is less
34 than seventy-five percent (75%) for any certification other than that of
35 a certificate for mine electrician, which requires a passing grade of not
36 less than eighty percent (80%). The board bureau shall observe the
37 requirements set forth in this section in conducting the examinations.
38 (b) (c) An applicant for a mine foreman certificate must have at
39 least four (4) years of experience underground in coal mines. However,
40 a person who has graduated and holds a degree in engineering or an
41 approved four (4) year program in coal mining technology from an
42 accredited school, college, or university is required to have only two (2)
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

263
1 years of practical underground mining experience to qualify for the
2 examination. A person who has graduated and holds a two (2) year
3 associate in applied science degree in coal mining technology from an
4 accredited school, college, or university is required to have only three
5 (3) years of practical underground mining experience to qualify for the
6 examination. An applicant must prove to the board bureau by written
7 and oral examination and by demonstration, where applicable, that the
8 applicant has a thorough knowledge of:
9 (1) the theory and practice of coal mining;
10 (2) the nature and properties of poisonous, noxious, and explosive
11 gases and methods for their detection and control;
12 (3) the requirements of the coal mining laws of this state; and
13 (4) the responsibilities and duties of a mine foreman under such
14 laws;
15 and that the applicant is otherwise qualified by law.
16 (c) (d) An applicant for a mine examiner certificate must have at
17 least three (3) years of experience underground in coal mines.
18 However, a person who has graduated and holds a degree in
19 engineering or an associate in applied science degree in coal mining
20 technology from an accredited school, college, or university is required
21 to have only two (2) years of practical underground mining experience
22 to qualify for the examination. An applicant must prove to the board
23 bureau by written and oral examination and by demonstration, where
24 applicable, that the applicant has a thorough knowledge of:
25 (1) the nature and properties of poisonous, noxious, and explosive
26 gases and methods for their detection and control;
27 (2) the practical aspects of coal mining pertaining especially to
28 ventilation and roof control; and
29 (3) the responsibilities of a mine examiner under coal mining laws
30 of this state;
31 and that the applicant is otherwise qualified by law.
32 (d) (e) An applicant for a shot-firer certificate must have at least one
33 (1) year of underground experience and must have been properly
34 trained in a course approved by the director in the safe use and
35 handling of explosives. An applicant must prove to the board bureau
36 by written and oral examination and by demonstration, where
37 applicable, that the applicant has a working knowledge of:
38 (1) the proper handling and use of explosives and blasting devices
39 and the danger connected therewith;
40 (2) the nature and properties of poisonous, noxious, and explosive
41 gases and methods for their detection;
42 (3) the coal mining laws of the state pertaining to ventilation, roof
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

264
1 control, and blasting; and
2 (4) the responsibilities of a shot-firer under applicable mining
3 laws;
4 and that the applicant is otherwise qualified by law.
5 (e) (f) An applicant for a hoisting engineer certificate must prove to
6 the board bureau by written and oral examination and by
7 demonstration, where applicable, that the applicant:
8 (1) is capable of operating a hoist;
9 (2) has a thorough knowledge of the coal mining laws of this state
10 pertaining to hoisting operations;
11 (3) has at least one (1) year mining experience;
12 (4) has at least twenty (20) hours practical experience under the
13 supervision of a certified hoisting engineer; and
14 (5) is otherwise qualified by law.
15 (f) (g) An applicant for a belt examiner certificate must have at least
16 one (1) year of experience in belt maintenance or installation work. The
17 applicant must prove to the board bureau by written and oral
18 examination and by demonstration, where applicable, that the applicant
19 has a thorough knowledge of:
20 (1) the requirements of the coal mining laws of this state with
21 particular emphasis upon those laws pertaining to the use of
22 electrical or belt equipment and the transmission of electrical
23 energy into coal mines; and
24 (2) the responsibilities of a belt examiner under those laws;
25 and that the applicant is otherwise qualified by law.
26 (g) (h) An applicant for a mine electrician certificate must have
27 sufficient (but not less than one (1) year of) experience in performing
28 electrical work. The applicant must prove to the board bureau by
29 written and oral examination and by demonstration, where applicable,
30 that the applicant has a thorough knowledge of:
31 (1) the requirements of the coal mining laws of this state, with
32 particular emphasis upon laws pertaining to electrical energy in
33 coal mines;
34 (2) direct and alternating current theory and application;
35 (3) electric equipment and electrical circuits in coal mines;
36 (4) permissibility of electric equipment;
37 (5) 30 CFR 75 subparts F-K; and
38 (6) 30 CFR 77 subparts F-J and S.
39 (h) (i) An applicant for an examination under this section must pay
40 the bureau of mines and mine safety an examination fee of twenty-five
41 dollars ($25). All fees collected under this subsection shall be
42 deposited in the mine safety fund established by IC 22-10-12-16. The
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

265
1 board bureau may set a different fee by rule under IC 22-10-1.5-4.
2 (i) (j) A mine foreman, mine examiner, shot-firer, hoisting engineer,
3 mine electrician, or belt examiner certificate issued before September
4 1, 1979, is valid under the mining laws of Indiana.
5 (j) (k) A person who was issued a fire-boss certificate before July 1,
6 2007, shall be issued a replacement mine examiner certificate upon
7 request to the director.
8 (k) (l) A person designated as mine superintendent or assistant mine
9 superintendent, or acting in either capacity, must hold a mine foreman
10 certificate.
11 (l) (m) A certificate may be granted to an applicant who presents to
12 the board satisfactory evidence that the applicant has not been
13 convicted of:
14 (1) an act which would constitute a ground for disciplinary
15 sanction under section 11.1(b) of this chapter; or
16 (2) a felony that has a direct bearing on the applicant's ability to
17 act competently as a mine foreman, shot-firer, mine examiner,
18 hoisting engineer, mine electrician, or belt examiner.
19 (m) (n) For the purpose of safety, the board bureau may refuse to
20 examine an applicant who cannot:
21 (1) readily understand the written English language; or
22 (2) express himself or herself in the English language.

267
1 (5) place a practitioner on probation status and require the

17 health and safety if he is allowed to continue to practice. The summary
18 suspension may be renewed upon a hearing before the board, bureau,
19 and each renewal may be for a period of ninety (90) days or less.

21 suspended upon the conviction of the practitioner of a felony under 30
22 U.S.C. 820. A practitioner whose certificate is suspended under this
23 subsection may apply for reinstatement under subsection (h) if at least
24 five (5) years have elapsed from the practitioner's date of discharge
25 from probation, imprisonment, or parole from the felony.
26 (h) The board bureau may reinstate a certificate which has been
27 suspended under this chapter if, after a hearing, the board bureau is
28 satisfied that the applicant is able to practice with reasonable skill and
29 safety. As a condition for reinstatement, the board bureau may impose
30 disciplinary or corrective measures authorized under this chapter.
31 (i) The board bureau shall seek to achieve consistency in the
32 application of the sanctions authorized in this section, and significant
33 departures from prior decisions involving similar conduct shall be
34 explained in the board's findings or orders.

20 department of homeland security.
21 (2) "Exit" means a continuous and unobstructed means of egress
22 to a public way designated as an exit pursuant to the rules of the
23 commission. department. The term includes doorways, corridors,
24 exterior exit balconies, ramps, stairways, smokeproof enclosures,
25 horizontal exits, exit passageways, exit courts, and yards.
26 (3) "Owner" means a person having control or custody of any
27 building covered by this chapter.
28 (4) "Public building" means any structure used in whole or in part
29 as a place of resort, assemblage, lodging, trade, traffic,
30 occupancy, or use by the public, or by three (3) or more tenants.
31 It also means all educational buildings, day care centers,
32 hospitals, institutions, health facilities, residential-custodial care
33 facilities, mercantile occupancies, and office occupancies.
34 (5) "Special egress control device" means an electronically
35 controlled exit locking system that:
36 (A) allows a delay in exiting through an exit in a
37 nonemergency situation; and
38 (B) complies with rules adopted by the commission.
39 department.
40 SECTION 459. IC 22-11-17-2, AS AMENDED BY P.L.187-2021,
41 SECTION 66, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
42 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 2. (a) Except as provided in subsections (b) and
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

277
1 (d) and section 2.5 of this chapter, an owner of a public building shall
2 not permit an exit to be locked or obstructed in any manner that denies
3 the public a continuous and unobstructed means of egress while
4 lawfully occupied by anyone who is not an officer or an employee.
5 (b) The commission department may adopt rules under IC 4-22-2
6 that:
7 (1) allow the owner of a public building to equip an exit with a
8 special egress control device;
9 (2) limit the circumstances under which a special egress control
10 device may be used; and
11 (3) allow an exit that was in compliance with the rules of the
12 commission department when the exit was constructed to be
13 equipped with a special egress control device.
14 (c) An owner of a public building shall not permit a fire alarm to be
15 disconnected or otherwise rendered inoperative, except in cases of
16 routine maintenance or for repair.
17 (d) A school that has one (1) or more employees shall develop a
18 plan to address unplanned fire alarm activation as part of its emergency
19 operations plan.
20 (e) A school's emergency operations plan for unplanned fire alarm
21 activation shall include procedures for the following:
22 (1) Evacuation of the building when the fire alarm is heard. A
23 school with a fire alarm panel that allows for a positive fire alarm
24 sequence may:
25 (A) develop a plan to investigate an unplanned fire alarm
26 activation before activating the audible and visual alarms
27 requiring evacuation;
28 (B) designate school officials to acknowledge that an alarm
29 has been activated and initiate an investigation within fifteen
30 (15) seconds;
31 (C) secure-in-place for up to three (3) minutes in order for a
32 designated school official to determine, by investigation, if an
33 active shooter is on the property; and
34 (D) following the three (3) minute period under clause (C), the
35 school must evacuate, unless an active shooter has been
36 verified to be on the school's property.
37 (2) Compliance with all provisions of 675 IAC 28-1-28.

13 SECTION 465. IC 22-12-2 IS REPEALED [EFFECTIVE JULY 1,
14 2026]. (Fire Prevention and Building Safety Commission).
15 SECTION 466. IC 22-12-2.1 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA
16 CODE AS A NEW CHAPTER TO READ AS FOLLOWS
17 [EFFECTIVE UPON PASSAGE]:
18 Chapter 2.1. Transition of Responsibilities for Fire Prevention
19 and Building Safety
20 Sec. 1. (a) On July 1, 2026, all powers, duties, agreements, and
21 liabilities of the fire prevention and building safety commission are
22 transferred to the department.
23 (b) On July 1, 2026, all records and property of the fire
24 prevention and building safety commission, including
25 appropriations and other funds under the control or supervision of
26 the fire prevention and building safety commission, are transferred
27 to the department.
28 (c) After June 30, 2026, any amounts owed to the fire prevention
29 and building safety commission before July 1, 2026, are considered
30 to be owed to the department as the successor agency.
31 (d) After June 30, 2026, a reference to the fire prevention and
32 building safety commission in a statute, rule, or other document is
33 considered a reference to the department.
34 (e) Proceedings pending before the fire prevention and building
35 safety commission on July 1, 2026, shall be transferred from the
36 fire prevention and building safety commission to the department
37 and treated as if initiated by the department.
38 (f) A license or permit issued by the fire prevention and building
39 safety commission before July 1, 2026, shall be treated after June
40 30, 2026, as a certification issued by the department.
41 (g) The rules adopted by the fire prevention and building safety
42 commission before July 1, 2026, are considered, after June 30,
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

280
1 2026, rules of the department. For purposes of applying IC 4-22-2.6
2 to the readoption of rules transferred by this subsection, the rules
3 shall be treated as not expiring until January 1, 2031. In the
4 interim, except as permitted under IC 4-22-2-38 to make
5 nonsubstantive technical corrections, the department may not
6 revise or repeal the transferred rules, including matters
7 incorporated by reference, until the rules are codified by the
8 general assembly or otherwise authorized by act of the general
9 assembly.
10 (h) Not later than July 1, 2027, and each year thereafter, the
11 department shall submit a report to the legislative council in an
12 electronic format under IC 5-14-6 making recommendations for
13 codification and amendment of the rules of the fire prevention and
14 building safety commission. The legislative council shall assign the
15 report to be reviewed by an study committee established under
16 IC 2-5-1.3. In preparing the report required by this subsection, the
17 department shall consult with industry experts, professionals, and
18 interested stakeholders who have indicated an interest in the
19 subject matter of the recommendations contained in the report.

10 commission adopts or updates after December 31, 2023. A unit may

12 acceptable to the commission. department. A proposal must include

14 (1) A resolution adopted by the unit to request an amendment to

18 amendment.

20 from the proposed building code amendment.

22 year to review and consider all proposals submitted by units at least
23 annually. A unit may submit a proposal at any time. However, the
24 commission shall review and consider only complete proposal
25 submissions that are received not later than sixty (60) days before the
26 date of a meeting in which proposals are considered.

30 considered; and

34 commission present and voting as provided in IC 22-12-2-6.

30 [EFFECTIVE JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 15. (a) This section applies to any

32 rule of the department.
33 (b) The department shall authorize compliance with a more
34 recent version of a model code than that which is currently

38 department must:
39 (1) specifically identify, by name and version, the model code

HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

298
1 or welfare of the public;
2 (3) identify any other associated model codes that must also be
3 complied with in lieu of the Indiana version; and
4 (4) file a notice in the Indiana Register containing the
5 information described in subdivisions (1) through (3).
6 (d) The department may impose conditions on an authorization
7 issued under this section necessary to ensure that the following are
8 maintained:
9 (1) The health, safety, or welfare of the public.
10 (2) The integrity of Indiana's regulatory program.
11 (e) The department may modify or eliminate any authorization
12 made under this section by filing an updated notice in the Indiana
13 Register. The modification or elimination of an authorization may
14 not take effect sooner than one hundred eighty (180) days after the
15 date of posting in the Indiana Register. The modification or
16 elimination of an authorization is subject to IC 4-21.5.
17 (f) Compliance with an authorization made under this section is
18 voluntary. However, if a person elects to comply with an
19 authorization, the failure to comply with any provision of the
20 authorization is a violation of the building, fire safety, or
21 equipment laws of Indiana.

16 and willful disregard for the public health, safety, or welfare.
17 (b) The provisions regarding the time limitations for review and
18 notice under this chapter do not apply, and the department is not
19 required to issue a design release and confirmation number for
20 providing notice. The department shall send written notice of its
21 determination to:
22 (1) the design professional's licensing or registration authority
23 under IC 25-4-1 or IC 25-31, IC 25-4-3, as appropriate, for the
24 purpose of conducting a hearing under IC 4-21.5 to determine if
25 action under IC 4-21.5-3-8 is appropriate;
26 (2) the design professional; and
27 (3) the project owner or general contractor on whose behalf the
28 application was submitted.
29 (c) An applicant that receives notice under subsection (b) may
30 withdraw the application and submit a new application and plans to the
31 department that are prepared by a different design professional.
32 Withdrawal of an application does not affect any disciplinary action
33 against the professional of record that prepared the plans described in
34 subsection (a).
35 SECTION 538. IC 22-15-3.5 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA
36 CODE AS A NEW CHAPTER TO READ AS FOLLOWS
37 [EFFECTIVE JULY 1, 2026]:
38 Chapter 3.5. Self-certification by Design Professional
39 Sec. 1. The definitions in IC 22-15-3.2 apply throughout this
40 chapter.
41 Sec. 2. (a) Notwithstanding any other law, an applicant for a
42 construction project requiring a design release for a Class 1
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

313
1 structure under IC 22-12-3 may satisfy the design release
2 requirement through self-certification.
3 (b) Self-certification may be used only for projects that are
4 required to be signed and sealed by an Indiana registered design
5 professional. The department may exclude projects from
6 self-certification that involve high-hazard occupancies, essential
7 facilities, or other structures determined to pose an elevated risk
8 to life safety.
9 Sec. 3. To self-certify a project, an applicant for a design release
10 must:
11 (1) be an Indiana registered design professional in good
12 standing with the applicable registration board;
13 (2) be a registered design professional for at least two (2)
14 years;
15 (3) have completed at least five (5) projects in Indiana as the
16 registered design professional of record or as the registered
17 design professional responsible for the production of the
18 construction documents; and
19 (4) maintain professional liability insurance of at least five
20 hundred thousand dollars ($500,000) per claim and one
21 million dollars ($1,000,000) aggregate.
22 Sec. 4. The department shall develop an electronic application
23 form for self-certifying applicants.
24 Sec. 5. The certifying design professional shall:
25 (1) perform a comprehensive review of the construction
26 documents; and
27 (2) submit to the department a signed certification, under
28 penalty of perjury, affirming that the plans comply with all
29 applicable state building codes and rules.
30 Sec. 6. Upon receipt of a complete certification and required
31 documentation, the department shall, not later than the next
32 business day, provide to the applicant:
33 (1) a copy of the design release; or
34 (2) a confirmation number that serves as a temporary design
35 release until the applicant receives a copy of the design
36 release;
37 without conducting a technical plan review.
38 Sec. 7. The department may:
39 (1) audit self-certified projects;
40 (2) require corrective action for non-compliance;
41 (3) revoke or suspend a professional's authority to provide
42 certifications; and
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

314
1 (4) refer violations to the appropriate licensing board or
2 impose civil penalties as authorized by law.
3 Sec. 8. The department may collect plan review fees for projects
4 that are released without a plan review.
5 Sec. 9. Nothing in this chapter limits the authority of state or
6 local inspection officials to conduct inspections or enforce
7 compliance during construction.
8 Sec. 10. An applicant who is qualified to self-certify may apply
9 for a design release under:
10 (1) this chapter; or
11 (2) IC 22-15-3.
12 Sec. 11. Participation in the self-certification program is
13 voluntary. Nothing in this chapter prohibits the department from
14 reviewing or not reviewing an application in accordance with
15 IC 22-15-3.

34 SECTION 547. IC 22-15-5-6, AS AMENDED BY P.L.2-2007,
35 SECTION 309, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS
36 [EFFECTIVE JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 6. (a) The following definitions
37 apply to sections 7 through 16 of this chapter:
38 (1) "Competency examination" means an examination that
39 thoroughly tests the scope of the knowledge and skill of the
40 applicant for the license.
41 (2) "Elevator apprentice" means an individual who works under
42 the direct supervision of a licensed elevator mechanic. The term
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

319
1 includes an individual commonly known as an elevator helper
2 while working under the direct supervision of a licensed elevator
3 mechanic.
4 (3) "Elevator contractor" means a person who alone or with other
5 persons, constructs, repairs, alters, remodels, adds to, subtracts
6 from, or improves a regulated lifting device and who is
7 responsible for substantially all the regulated lifting devices
8 within the entire project, or who fabricates elevator lifting devices
9 substantially completed and ready for installation.
10 (4) "Elevator inspector" means an individual who conducts the
11 acceptance inspection of a regulated lifting device required by
12 section 4(c)(1)(A) of this chapter.
13 (5) "Elevator mechanic" means an individual who engages in the
14 construction, reconstruction, alteration, maintenance, mechanical,
15 or electrical work or adjustments of a regulated lifting device.
16 (6) "License" means a certificate issued by the department that
17 confers upon the holder the privilege to act as an elevator
18 contractor, elevator inspector, or elevator mechanic.
19 (7) "Licensing program" means the program for licensing elevator
20 contractors, elevator inspectors, and elevator mechanics
21 established under this section and sections 7 through 16 of this
22 chapter.
23 (8) "Municipality" has the meaning set forth in IC 36-1-2-11.
24 (9) "Person" means:
25 (A) a natural person;
26 (B) the partners or members of a partnership or a limited
27 partnership;
28 (C) a state educational institution; or
29 (D) a corporation or the officers, directors, and employees of
30 the corporation.
31 (10) "Practitioner" means a person that holds:
32 (A) an unlimited license;
33 (B) a limited or probationary license;
34 (C) a temporary license;
35 (D) an emergency license; or
36 (E) an inactive license.
37 (b) The commission and the department shall establish a program
38 to license elevator contractors, elevator mechanics, and elevator
39 inspectors.
40 (c) The department shall issue a license as an elevator contractor, an
41 elevator mechanic, or an elevator inspector to a person who qualifies
42 and complies with the provisions of the licensing program. A person
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

320
1 who receives a license under this chapter is subject to the supervision
2 and control of the department.
3 (d) The department may contract with public and private
4 institutions, agencies, businesses, and organizations to implement all
5 or part of its duties established under this chapter.
6 (e) The commission department may adopt rules under IC 4-22-2
7 to implement the licensing program.

14 SECTION 551. IC 22-15-5-15 IS AMENDED TO READ AS
15 FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 15. (a) This section
16 does not apply to a licensed elevator contractor that is not an
17 individual.
18 (b) To renew a license issued under this licensing program, the
19 license holder must satisfy the continuing education requirement and
20 submit a proof of completion of training to the department.
21 (c) The continuing education requirement is at least eight (8) hours
22 of instruction that must be attended and completed within one (1) year
23 before a license renewal.
24 (d) The continuing education courses designed to ensure the
25 continuing education of an individual holding a license regarding new
26 and existing provisions of the rules of the commission department
27 may include:
28 (1) programs sponsored by the commission; department;
29 (2) trade association seminars;
30 (3) labor training programs; or
31 (4) joint labor management apprenticeship and journeyman
32 upgrade training programs.
33 For an individual's completion of a continuing education course to
34 satisfy the individual's continuing education requirement under this
35 chapter, the continuing education provider, instructor and the
36 curriculum must have been approved by the department.
37 (e) All instructors of continuing education courses must be approved
38 by the department. If an instructor is approved by the department, has
39 worked as an instructor teaching a curriculum approved by the
40 department at any time within the year preceding the expiration date of
41 the license, and submits proof of this work to the department, the
42 instructor is exempt from the requirements of subsection (c).
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

325
1 (f) Continuing education providers shall keep uniform records of
2 attendance at approved continuing education courses for at least ten
3 (10) years on forms designed and distributed by the department.
4 (g) A license holder who is unable to complete the continuing
5 education required under this chapter before the expiration of the
6 individual's license due to temporary physical or mental disability may
7 apply for a waiver from the department in accordance with the
8 following:
9 (1) A waiver application must be submitted to the department on
10 a form established by the department.
11 (2) A waiver application must be signed and accompanied by an
12 affidavit signed by the physician of the applicant attesting to the
13 applicant's temporary disability.
14 (h) After the cessation of the temporary disability, the applicant
15 must submit to the department a certification from the same physician,
16 if the physician is still the treating physician of the applicant, or from
17 a subsequent treating physician attesting to the termination of the
18 temporary disability.
19 (i) Upon the submission of the certification under subsection (h), the
20 department shall issue a temporary waiver of the continuing education
21 requirement. A temporary waiver is valid for ninety (90) days after the
22 date of issue and allows the individual to work as an elevator
23 contractor, elevator inspector, or elevator mechanic without the
24 completion of the continuing education requirement for ninety (90)
25 days.
26 (j) A temporary waiver of the continuing education requirement may
27 not be renewed.
28 SECTION 552. IC 22-15-5-16, AS AMENDED BY P.L.186-2025,
29 SECTION 123, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS
30 [EFFECTIVE JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 16. (a) A practitioner shall comply

31 SECTION 596. IC 25-4-0.5 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA CODE
32 AS A NEW CHAPTER TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
33 JANUARY 1, 2027]:
34 Chapter 0.5. Definitions
35 Sec. 1. The definitions in this chapter apply throughout this
36 article.
37 Sec. 2. "Approved engineering curriculum" means an
38 engineering curriculum of four (4) years or more that has been
39 approved by the board. In approving the engineering curriculum,
40 the board may take into consideration the standards of
41 accreditation adopted by the Engineering Accreditation
42 Commission of the Accreditation Board for Engineering and
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

343
1 Technology.
2 Sec. 3. "Board" refers to the Indiana board of registration for
3 design professionals established by IC 25-4-1-2.
4 Sec. 4. "Engineering intern" means an individual who:
5 (1) is a graduate from an approved engineering curriculum of
6 four (4) years or more or who has acquired, through
7 engineering education and experience in engineering work,
8 knowledge and skill approximating that obtained by
9 graduation in an approved engineering curriculum of four (4)
10 years or more;
11 (2) has successfully passed an examination as prescribed in
12 IC 25-4-3-5; and
13 (3) has been issued by the board an appropriate certificate of
14 enrollment as an engineering intern.
15 Sec. 5. "Landscape architecture" means the practice of
16 professional services such as consultation, investigation,
17 reconnaissance, research, planning, design, or responsible
18 supervision to develop land areas for the dominant purpose of
19 preserving, enhancing, or determining:
20 (1) proper land uses;
21 (2) natural land features;
22 (3) ground cover and planting;
23 (4) naturalistic and aesthetic values;
24 (5) the settings and approaches to structures or other
25 improvements;
26 (6) the natural environment of a facility, an individual
27 building, or other structure;
28 (7) site specific natural surface and subsoil drainage systems;
29 (8) landscape grading, swales, curbs, and walkways; and
30 (9) any inherent problems of the land relating to erosion,
31 overuse, blight, or other hazards.
32 The term includes the location and arrangement of the proposed
33 tangible objects and features that are incidental and necessary to
34 accomplish the purposes of landscape architecture.
35 Sec. 6. "Licensing agency" means the Indiana professional
36 licensing agency established by IC 25-1-5-3.
37 Sec. 7. "Practice of engineering" means any service or creative
38 work that the adequate performance of requires engineering
39 education, training, and experience in the application of special
40 knowledge of the mathematical, physical, and engineering sciences
41 to services or creative work that includes the following:
42 (1) Consultation.
HB 1003—LS 6756/DI 92

344
1 (2) Investigation.
2 (3) Evaluation.
3 (4) Planning, including planning the use of land and water.
4 (5) The design of or the supervision of the design of
5 engineering works and systems.
6 (6) Engineering surveys and studies or the supervision of
7 engineering surveys and studies, including all surveying
8 activities required to support the sound conception, planning,
9 design, construction, maintenance, and operation of
10 engineered projects, but not including the surveying of real
11 property for the establishment of land boundaries,
12 subdivisions, rights-of-way, easements, and the dependent or
13 independent surveys or resurveys of the public land survey
14 system.
15 (7) Evaluation of construction for the purpose of assuring
16 compliance with specifications, plans, and designs, in
17 connection with any public or private utilities, structures,
18 buildings, machines, equipment, processes, work systems, or
19 projects.
20 The term "practice of engineering" does not include the work
21 ordinarily performed by persons who operate or maintain
22 machinery or equipment.
23 Sec. 8. "Practice or offer to practice engineering" means the act
24 of an individual or a business who by verbal claim, sign,
25 advertisement, letterhead, card, telephone listing, or in any other
26 way represents the individual or the business to be a professional
27 engineer or who performs, or offers to perform, any acts or work
28 involving the practice of engineering.
29 Sec. 9. "Practitioner" means an individual registered as a
30 landscape architect under this article.
31 Sec. 10. "Professional engineer" means an individual who, by
32 reason of that individual's special knowledge of the mathematical
33 and physical sciences and the principles and methods of
34 engineering analysis and design which are acquired by education
35 and practical experience, is qualified to engage in the practice of
36 engineering as attested by that individual's registration as a
37 professional engineer.
